content:
primary source legal protection scout uniform, 1935: tanganyika government ordinance [official document] annotation many african boys, teachers, community leaders genuinely inspired scouting founded unauthorized independent troops. cases, individuals dressed scouts claim benefits belonging movement. scout leaders government officials east africa paid little attention informal adaptations scouting, became alarmed dance societies mombasa began use scout uniforms early 1930s. decidedly adult celebrations often included drinking forms inappropriate revelry. scout leaders lobbied colonial authorities crack scout impersonators, time laws unauthorized use scout materials east african colonies. legislation tanganyika (modern tanzania) gave territorial scout association sole legal control scout uniform badges. 1935 africans impersonated scouts formed unauthorized troops faced stiff fine one month jail time hard labor. source tanzania national archives, dar es salaam, tanzania. acc 71. annotated tim parsons. primary source text

an ordinance protect activities interests boy scouts association tanganyika territory, 5th july 1935.

  1. 1. ordinance may cited boy scouts ordinance.
  2. 2. ordinance. "association" means boy scouts association incorporated royal charter granted fourth day january, 1912.
  3. 3. shall lawful person, rules association duly authorized entitled publicly wear, carry bear uniform, badge, token emblem rules specifically adopted use authority association could reasonably held imitation style manner convey impression person rules entitled wear, carry bear uniform, badge, token emblem.
  4. 4. person shall sell, offer sale, article bearing badge token emblem specifically adopted use authority association, could reasonably held imitation unless shall first obtained authority commissioner writing so.
  5. 5. shall lawful boy scout, otherwise thereunto lawfully entitled authorised, pretend pass arrogate authority, position powers claim act
    1. (a) member tanganyika territory police force, or
    2. (b) officer exercising police functions service native authority
    3. (c) agent officer government native authority tribunal.
  6. 6. shall lawful person form, organise, work connection concerned forming, organising, working connection corps body persons without due authority granted accordance rules association claim purport boy scouts otherwise connected association hold pass boy scouts otherwise connected association.
  7. 7. person contravening provisions ordinance guilty offence liable imprisonment one month fine two hundred shillings penalties.

son chil necha blumstein (statement taken professor chaimówna, bedzin) mother sent away forced labour, next day father handed włada brought left ghetto, 23 june 1943, 10 november 1944, stayed flat whole time, mostly bed. visitors would walk around room, quietly one would hear. day one home went work. mr liwer stayed hidden home. elderly man. read newspapers books together, mostly religious ones others enough eat; father left money enough work, except time time peeled potatoes stoked stove. days terrible, sitting whole time without moving stuffy room; worst time fell ill needed doctor. went sosnowiec see doctor; terrified, prayed one would recognize doctor's pretended lady's son. doctor worms, wrote prescription two weeks later another trip like doctor's; time everything went smoothly. 10 november 1944 went live uncle lady knew village bonowice. uprising warsaw finished could take child whose parents died uprising. came back life. free. could go wherever wanted simply gulped air. one day lady came uncle's suspected jewish. fired lots questions able answer knew religion well; decided jewish. liberated russians. stayed country till 1 may 1945. went school; popular happy. times day. ate things sometimes gave bath, children home. winter, lay quilt day. don't remember whether cold. ill caught chill. never went opening hide-out, though could hear children playing garden; always remember one see afraid alone hide-out, never cried. missed mother father much. first days, father moved aunt's flat barracks jews living. time continued work railway. one day mr łopatyński content: primary source dolben's act 1788 [government document] annotation dolben's act 1788 proposed noted abolitionist sir william dolben english parliament. meant restrict slave trade, actually adverse effect children. act mandated two fifths ship's cargo children, also limited number african men 1 male per ship ton. restrictions threatening slave supply, planter demand began change response. since act define 'child,' children ages 12 18 entered trade. furthermore, act sparked important debate benefits breeding slaves rather buying consequently, act somewhat responsible increased number girls children trade. source donnan, elizabeth. documents illustrative slave trade america. volume 2. new york: octagon books, 1965, 583-87. annotated colleen vasconcellos. primary source text act regulate, limited time, shipping carrying slaves british vessels coast africa. whereas expedient regulate shipping carrying slaves british vessels coast africa; therefore enacted. shall lawful master, person taking charge command british ship vessel whatever, shall clear port kingdom first day august one thousand seven hundred eighty eight, board, one time, convey, carry, bring, transport slaves coast africa parts beyond sea, ship vessel, greater number proportion five slaves every three tons burthen ship vessel, burthen ship vessel, far ship vessel shall exceed two hundred one tons; moreover, one slave every additional ton ship vessel, burthen two hundred one tons, male slaves shall exceed four feet four inches height, greater number proportion one male slave every one ton burthen ship vessel, far ship vessel shall exceed two hundred one tons, three male slaves shall exceed height four feet four inches) every additional five tons ship vessel, burthen two hundred one tons. master, person taking charge command ship vessel, shall act contrary hereto, master, person aforesaid, shall forfeit pay sum thirty pounds lawful money great britain, every slave exceeding number proportions herein-before limited. ii. provided always, shall ship vessel, two fifth parts slaves shall children, shall exceed four feet four inches height, every five children aforesaid proportion two fifths) shall deemed taken equal four slaves within true intent meaning act. viii. person hindering process ascertaining number negroes vessel fined £100. xi. enacted authority aforesaid. shall lawful person become master, take command charge ship vessel time shall clear port great britain, purchasing carrying slaves coast africa, unless master, person taking charge command ship vessel, shall already served capacity one voyage, shall served chief mate surgeon whole two voyages, either chief mate, three voyages, purchasing carrying slaves coast africa; pain master, person taking charge command ship vessel, also owner owners, shall hire employ person, shall, every offence respectively, forfeit pay sum fifty pounds. xiv. enacted authority aforesaid…that shall died proportion two slaves hundred, time arrival ship vessel coast africa, time arrival port discharge islands west indies, belonging dominion majesty, case, collector principal officer aforesaid shall, hereby authorised required make certificates, specifying number slaves appear taken board ship vessel, number died within period mentioned; one certificates shall delivered master, surgeon ship vessel; production certificates, commissioners majesty's customs england scotland respectively shall, hereby authorised required direct sum one hundred pounds paid master, sum fifty pounds paid surgeon ship vessel, money shall hands receiver general customs england scotland respectively; shall made appear collector, principal officer aforesaid, shall died proportion three slaves hundred, time arrival ship vessel coast africa, time arrival port discharge west india islands, case collector principal officer aforesaid shall, hereby authorised required make like certificates, deliver one master, surgeon ship vessel; title: Children and Youth in History | Gin Lane (1751) [Engraving]
content: primary source gin lane (1751) [engraving] annotation one best-known prints famous artist, william hogarth. designed support british government's attempt regulate price popularity drinking gin (known geneva) gin act 1751. print accompanied following verse: gin, cursed fiend, fury fraught, makes human race prey. enters deadly draught steal life away. virtue truth, driv'n despair rage compells fly, cherishes hellish care theft, murder, perjury. damned cup! vitals preys liquid fire contains, madness heart conveys, rolls thro' veins. gin originally marketed medicine upset stomachs netherlands. imported britain 1689 quickly became choice drink poor. many distilled sold homes. historians claim 1750, one every fifteen houses london sold children would often sent buy gin parents sampled also regularly given calm babies. hogarth shows poverty, public drunkenness, crime, resulted cheap availability gin. shocking figure gin lane drunken mother. may partially based real person, judith dufour. due mother's neglect, two-year old child taken placed workhouse. dufour reclaimed child shortly afterwards strangled left body ditch. sold clothes workhouse provided child pennies, used money buy gin. dufour publicly hanged murder. source gin lane (1751). etching engraving william hogarth. print collection, miriam ira d. wallach division art, prints photographs. new york public library, astor, lenox tilden foundations. reproduced online: gin lane, norton anthology english literature, http://www.wwnorton.com/college/english/nael/18century/topic_1/illustrations/imginlane.htm (accessed march 26, 2008). annotated lynda payne. cite source william hogarth, "gin lane (1751) [engraving]," children youth history, item #158, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/158 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags gin lane, 1750-1914, engravings, europe, gin act 1751, william hogarth related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

title: Children and Youth in History | London's Bill of Mortality (December 1664-December 1665) [Official Document]
content: primary source london's bill mortality (december 1664-december 1665) [official document] annotation great outbreak bubonic plague black death hot summer 1665 london, special bills mortality issued listed causes death. mid-july thousand deaths week reported handbills stuck public places warn people plague growing. rich fled city poor option died droves. shown front bill lists final count year 1665 memento mori remember die, written across top skeletons representing death around edges. second bill lists number deaths london one week september 1665. shows 7,165 people died plague. deaths recorded point high infant mortality early modern england; 17 chrisomes, infants died first month life; 121 teeth, infants died still teething. fifteen children died worms parasites body. several fevers also mentioned 42 women died childbed fever, bacterial infection giving birth, 101 people succumbed spotted fever (probably typhus). source wellcome library, "london's dreadful visitation: collection bills mortality present year: beginning 27th december 1664 ending 19th december following: also general whole years bill. according report made king's excellent majesty company parish-clerks london," wellcome library, wellcome collection, http://images.wellcome.ac.uk/ixbin/hixclient.exe?miropac=l0030700 http://images.wellcome.ac.uk/ixbin/hixclient.exe?miropac=l0030701 (accessed october 1, 2008). annotated lynda payne. cite source "london's bill mortality (december 1664-december 1665) [official document]," children youth history, item #159, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/159 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags 1450-1770, black death, bubonic plague, europe, government documents, london's bill mortality, official documents related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

title: Children and Youth in History | John Evelyn's Diary, 1658 [Literary Excerpt]
content: primary source john evelyn's diary, 1658 [literary excerpt] annotation english lawyer john evelyn (1620-1706) kept diary nearly 50 years recorded grief death four children. john mary evelyn eight children altogether: richard (1652–8), john standsfield (1653–4), john (1655–99), george (1657–8), richard (1664), mary (1665–85), elizabeth (1667–85) susanna (1669–1754). susanna outlived parents. january 1658 eldest son dick (richard) fell ill quartan ague fever, sweats fits, finally died. physicians sent london bitterly cold weather prevented arriving time help little dick. evelyn's devastation loss son shows well painfully records dick's age 5 years, 5 months, 3 days. angrily blames death servants keeping dick hot great fire blankets. evelyn educated man interested science natural philosophy called 17th century. may led somewhat unusual decision attend autopsy dick. findings liver growne large spleen, suggest possibly rickets malaria cause dick's death. following month evelyn recorded death yet another son youngest, george, aged seven weeks. source john evelyn, "1658," geocities, http://www.geocities.com/paris/leftbank/1914/ed_1658.html#1658 (accessed march 28, 2008). annotated lynda payne. primary source text 1658 january 27 six fitts quartan ague pleased god visite deare child dick fitts extreame, especiale one sides, rigor hot fitt, fell greate intollerable sweate, surpriz'd aboundance vapours ascending head, fell fatal symptoms, help hand able recover spirits, long painefull conflict, falling sleep thought, coverd warme, (though midst severe frosty season) greate fire roome; plainely expired, unexpressable griefe affliction. sent physitians lond, whilst yet life river frozen coach brake way ere got mile house; artificial help failing, natural strength exhausted, lost prettiest, dearest child, ever parents 5 years <5 months> 3 days old years even tender age, aprodigie witt, understanding; beauty body angel, endowments mind, incredible rare hopes. 30 saturday following, sufferd physitians opened: dr. needham dr. welles, come three days little time ere expired, past help, opinion suffocated woman maide tended covered hott blankets lay cradle, neere excessive hot fire close roome; wife long come come lifting blanket, quite coved cradle, taking first notice wonderfull fresh colour, hardly hearing breath heave, soone perceived neere overcome heate sweate, doubtlesse child farr gon, could make heare, open eyes, though life apparently gave something make sneeze ineffectivly: open'd found membranous substance growing cavous part liver, somewhat neere edge compasse 3 inches, ought liver fixed onely three strong ligaments, far distant part; confidently affirm'd, child tis vulgarly cald) liver-growne, thence sicknesse frequent complaint side: indeede liver splen exceedingly large &c: caused body cofin'd lead reposited night, 8 clock church deptford, accompanied divers relations neighbours, among distributed rings ___ dominus abstulit: intending (god willing) transported owne body, interrd dormitorie wotton churh deare native county surry, lay bones mingle dust fathers 1 god gracious make fit blessed child ends joy life, go even mourning grave: l. jesus sanctifie others afflictions: amen: february 15 afflicting hand god still upon us, pleased also take away us morning youngest sonn george 7 weeks languishing nurse, breeding teeth, ending dropsie: gods holy buried deptford church 17th following:____ march ben severest winter, man alive knowne england: crowes feet frozen prey: ilands ice inclosed fish foule frozen, persons boates: 1 richard's body never moved. epitaph still st. nicholas, deptford. e gave similar account son's life preface translation golden book john chrysostom, see p. 119 459. 2 foeffees 'trustees holding land charitable uses' (de beer). 3'with great pomp', acts apostles, xxv.23. e's translation, see p. 459. cite source john evelyn, "john evelyn's diary, 1658 [literary excerpt]," children youth history, item #160, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/160 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags 1450-1770, diaries, england, europe, john evelyn, mary evelyn, personal accounts related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

content: primary source rubeola vulgaris (measles) [still image] annotation robert willan (1757-1812) physician practiced london. like sydenham fascinated relation weather epidemics kept strict records occurred several years. particularly interested diseases children carefully observed rashes pustules developed stages skin. first volume book, cutaneous diseases, published 1798-1808 widely admired medical world. historians generally agree book launched modern specialty dermatology. volume notable beautiful graphic colored plates. willan closely supervised creation 1812 preparing second volume publication, willan sadly died tuberculosis. plate 20 shows young child's face arm covered rash characteristic measles. often severe disfiguring disease early modern england could result death infection spread tissue bone, resulting gangrene. source willan, robert. cutaneous diseases. n.p., 1808. wellcome collection, "pate 20: cutaneous diseases," wellcome library, http://library.wellcome.ac.uk/doc_wtd015575.html (accessed october 13, 2008). cite source "rubeola vulgaris (measles) [still image]," children youth history, item #161, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/161 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags on cutaneous diseases, 1750-1914, europe, literary sources, paintings prints, robert willan related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

title: Children and Youth in History | Infanticide Trial Transcript from the Old Bailey of Elizabeth Taylor of Clerkenwell, London, June 1734 [Trial Record]
content: primary source infanticide trial transcript old bailey elizabeth taylor clerkenwell, london, june 1734 [trial record] annotation infanticide killing baby punishable hanging early modern england. unlike married women accused infanticide, mere fact single women tried conceal death babies considered proof murder infanticide act 1624. single woman's recourse try prove baby born dead killed difficult many women gave birth witnesses. mid-18th century research historians, know 70% women indicted infanticide servants occupation age 16. also know trend century progressed juries find women innocent guilty crime infanticide unless clear proof murdered baby. perhaps beginning see young women victims rather criminals. case elizabeth taylor shows fear shame single women felt became pregnant lack privacy lives servants. elizabeth speak trial, instead witnesses prosecution called first judge jury ask questions whether elizabeth hid pregnancy baby. dinah beaven testifies baby appear murdered. probably midwife acts expert witness. finally, prisoner newgate elizabeth states found baby items sewn elizabeth's coat prisoners took (new prisoners often fleeced inmates used money goods bribe gaolers providing basics bad bread, foul water, old straw bedding.) fact elizabeth made provision baby strongest proof intend kill court let go. [full text available online.] source may, allyson n. first denied infanticide trials old bailey." women history: voices early modern england, edited valerie frith, 31–2. concord, ontario: irwin publishing, 1997. annotated lynda payne. primary source text june 1734 elizabeth turner, clerkenwell, indicted murder male bastard infant, strangling hands, april 12. eleanor turnly. prisoner servant mrs windsor, pastry-cook, st. john's lane. margaret goldsmith, came lodge little ladyday [25 march, beginning year old calendar], observed prisoner looked big, easter, looked lank. suspected delivered, though appeared publicly every day. never heard cry could think become child. short, thought family alike, things could kept private. watched harkened ever could. fancied child might nurse garret [attic], often whipping stairs. could find nothing, concluded baked oven. last mrs. goldsmith, going cellar, came told husband, seen wig-box smelled something. went came like dead man, put hand box, felt child, surprised take consulted says kept thing hugger-mugger [secret], won't let 'em know child found fetch constable [policeman]. mr. goldsmith fetched constable watch [man watched streets prevent crimes], brought child mouldy. prisoner, first, denied child; little time owned juryman. seemed diligent watching prisoner. ever tax [question] child, child found. turnly. never spoke word could bear sight creature. margaret thomas goldsmith, deposed effect. elizabeth windsor. prisoner never told child, ill, great deal water come much better. child first found, denied owned afterwards. dinah beaven. child crowded box putrefied. full time. could discern mark violence. small wound head; known thing happen honest woman's child; fell want assistance. sarah hawkey. prisoner brought newgate, prisoners took coat, garnish money. found baby things, sewed coat. here's shirt, cap, stay tie clothing?], forehead-cloth, biggin tight fitting cap]. jury acquitted cite source "infanticide trial transcript old bailey elizabeth taylor clerkenwell, london, june 1734 [trial record]," children youth history, item #162, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/162 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags infanticide act 1624, 1450-1770, dinah beaven, elizabeth taylor, europe, infanticide, legal documents related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

title: Children and Youth in History | The Graham Children (1742) [Painting]
content: primary source graham children (1742) [painting] annotation beautiful life-size painting four children william hogarth, also specialized engravings gin lane. commissioned daniel graham, rich apothecary (pharmacist) royal family chelsea hospital london. shows daniel's three children second wife, mary crisp, mary's daughter, henrietta, first marriage. children left right) infant thomas sitting gold-leaf gilded go-cart (age 2); henrietta blue dress holding two cherries (age 9); anna maria flower-print dress (age 5); richard plays sérinette bird organ (age 7). hogarth patron foundling hospital london painted portraits many children skill led commissions one. first glance hogarth literally paints childhood time innocence happiness. clues point fragility children's lives. clock mantelpiece decorated figure cupid holding scythe, symbol death. standing beside hourglass, yet another symbol mortality. cat gazes greedily caged bird remind us predators even happy scene fact thomas died hogarth completing portrait posthumous representation infant, still alive age two. crossed carnations thomas's feet symbolize like flowers, life faded quickly, dove go-cart may represent soul flying heaven. source hogarth, william. graham children. oil canvas, 1742. annotated lynda payne. cite source william hogarth, graham children (1742) [painting]," children youth history, item #163, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/163 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags the graham children, 1450-1770, daniel graham, europe, mary crisp, paintings prints, william hogarth related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

title: Children and Youth in History | Transplanting Teeth (c.1790) [Engraving]
content: primary source transplanting teeth (c.1790) [engraving] annotation print thomas rowlandson (1756-1827) dated 1787. satirical comment upon real practice rich gentlemen ladies 18th century paying teeth pulled poor children transplanted gums. dentist present portrayed quack. even two quacking ducks placard advertising fake credentials. busy pulling teeth mouth poor young chimney sweep. covered soot exhausted, slumps chair. meanwhile dentist's assistant transplants tooth fashionably dressed young lady's mouth. two children seen leaving room clutching faces obviously pain teeth extracted. people lost teeth age 21 due gum disease, teeth transplants popular time england although rarely worked. source thomas rowlandson, "transplanting teeth," wellcome library, http://images.wellcome.ac.uk/indexplus/result.html?_ixfirst_=9&_ixss_=_ixfirst_%3d1%26_ixaction_%3dquery%26_ixmaxhits_%3d15%26%2524%2bwith%2bwi_sfgu%2bis%2by%3d%252e%26with%2bimage_sort%3d%252e%26_ixsession_%3dcrhmb4nhlbn%26create_creator_name_name%253atext%3d%2522thomas%2browlandson%2522%26%2524%2bnot%2b%2522contemporary%2bclinical%2bimages%2522%2bindex%2bwi_collection%3d%252e%26_ixfpfx_%3dtemplates%252ft&_ixaction_=query&_ixmaxhits_=1&_ixsr_=npsltgdywbk&_ixspfx_=templates%2ft&_ixfpfx_=templates%2ft (accessed october 13, 2008). annotated lynda payne. cite source thomas rowlandson, "transplanting teeth (c.1790) [engraving]," children youth history, item #164, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/164 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated lynda payne tags transplanting teeth, 1750-1914, engravings, europe, thomas rowlandson related contenthealth england (16th–18th c.)

content: primary source decameron [literary excerpt] annotation giovanni boccaccio provided famous description happened black death italy. report behavior florentines plague entered city spring 1348 serves introduction frame collection 100 tales entitled decameron. epidemic provides pretext group young men women leave florence retire pleasant villa countryside entertain telling stories 10 days. though novella range bawdy pious, story telling light hearted keeping purpose restorative diversion. boccaccio's introduction, however, different tone. author relates precise detail gruesome symptoms disease horrific circumstances took place florence epidemic swept town disrupting forms normal human relations. government, medical care, neighborliness broke people secluded restricted diet, others recklessly gave pleasure, still others tried behave temperately. distressing according boccaccio, collapse families abandonment children. english translation followed original italian. source boccaccio, giovanni. decameron. translated g. h. mcwilliam. 2nd ed. london: penguin books, 1995, 8–9. annotated shona kelly wray. primary source text merely question one citizen avoiding another, people almost invariably neglecting neighbours rarely never visiting relatives, addressing distance; scourge implanted great terror hearts men women brothers abandoned brothers, uncles nephews, sisters brothers, many cases wives deserted husbands. even worse, almost incredible fact fathers mothers refused nurse assist children, though belong [original italian] e lasciamo stare che l'uno cittadino l'altro schifasse e quasi niuno vicino avesse dell'altro cura e parenti insieme rade volte o non mai si visitassero e di lontano: era con sí fatto spavento questa tribulazione entrata ne' petti degli uomini e delle donne, che l'un fratello l'altro abbandonava e il zio il nepote e la sorella il fratello e spesse volte la donna il suo marito; e, che maggior cosa e quasi non credibile, li padri e lemadri figliuoli, quasi loro non fossero, di visitare e di servire schifavano. cite source giovanni boccaccio, "decameron [literary excerpt]," children youth history, item #177, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/177 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated shona kelly wray tags decameron, 1000-1500 ce, europe, giovanni boccaccio, italy, literary sources related contentchildren black death

content: primary source health ordinances pistoia, 1348 [legal document] annotation cities italy passed legislation aimed preventing reducing effects plague. since scientific view plague caused miasma bad air, measures targeted rotting smelling matter, viz. cloth could retain miasma spread disease passed person person, dead bodies, rotting meat. thus, ordinances regulated burials restricted mobility cloth activities butchers tanners. ordinances really new black death; governments simply re-enforced sanitary legislation effect cities 13th century. source chiappelli, alberto, ed. title: Children and Youth in History | Child’s sock [Object]
content: primary source child’s sock [object] annotation archaeologist w. m. flinders petrie found child's sock, dated 2nd century c.e., cemetery oxyrhyncus, greek monastic centre banks nile egypt. sock made wool yarn technique called "sprang," loop knitting, short pieces yarn looped circular pattern using needle. sock features separate section large toe likely worn thong sandal made woven rushes leather strap sole. example unusual skill egyptian maker elaborate multi-colored, striped pattern. rare common objects preserved archaeological record subject wear life disintegration burial. egypt's dry climate care taken burials preserved technique loop knitting widespread ancient world, identified well-preserved textile examples peru, denmark, greece. technique sometimes involved use frame, used make hairnets, hoods, fishnets well. source manchester museum, university manchester, uk. photo geoff thompson. cited harris, jennifer, ed. textiles: 5,000 years. london new york: trustees british museum/harry n. abrams, 1993. loop knitting diagram dorothy burnham, illustration (page 16) information pack textiles petrie museum egyptian archaeology http://www.petrie.ucl.ac.uk/index2.html. cite source "child’s sock [object]," children youth history, item #208, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/208 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags 1000 bce-300 ce, artifacts/objects, egypt, middle east/north africa, mortality, oxyrhyncus, w. m. flinders petrie related contentinfant's tunics [object]childrens tunics [object]

title: Children and Youth in History | Ivory doll [Toy]
content: primary source ivory doll [toy] annotation finely carved ivory doll moveable arms legs found grave girl approximately five years age tarragona, spain, port city south barcelona. dates 3rd 4th century ce. girl's skeleton adorned gold thread around neck along length, probably border burial dress. doll well proportioned, detailed carving face elaborate hairstyle. doll's limbs jointed shoulders, elbows, hips, knees, could assumed many positions active play. found 1927, child's grave one 12 among 2,000 graves necropolis contained funerary artifacts. justinian law period restricted placing offerings graves prevent desecration graves robbers. unusual presence doll may indicate child especially dear family, attached doll life. source ivory doll [mnat(p)12906], early christian necropolis (necròpolis paleocristiana), museu nacional arquelogic de tarragona (spain), class="external" href="http://www.mnat.es/new/gener98/cat/index.html">http://www.mnat.es/new/gener98/cat/index.html (accessed march 5, 2009). cite source "ivory doll [toy]," children youth history, item #209, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/209 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags 300-1000 ce, artifacts/objects, burial, byzantium, dolls, europe, material culture, rome, toys

title: Children and Youth in History | Infant's Tunics [Object]
content: primary source infant's tunics [object] annotation two infant tunics, found south cairo archaeologists, date period arab conquest egypt. first tunic, measuring 45 centimeters long 47 cm wide (17.7 x 18.5 inches), made single length hand-woven brown wool folded half shoulders sewn sides, leaving openings arms vents hand-stitched hem mobility. stripe orange woven across shoulders applied band sewn onto neck opening. tunic features rows cream red woolen pile loops warmth. tunic buried loops facing pile likely worn inside life. second tunic, 46 cm (18.1 inches) 48 cm (18.9 inches), also made single length cloth folded two. neck finishing cut cloth sewn onto tunic white linen thread, along band red wool geometric patterning. tunic may recycled adult garment, heavily repaired. approximately 11 different shades wool thread darned base fabric rectangular patterns, restoring worn object making plain one colorful. magnified detail available online tunic 1 tunic 2. source children's tunics, [t.8380 t.8505], whitworth art gallery, university manchester, u.k., class="external" href="http://www.whitworth.manchester.ac.uk/collection/advsearch/objdispextra/index.html?irn=22412">http://www.whitworth.manchester.ac.uk/collection/advsearch/objdispextra/index.html?irn=22412 (tunic 1, t.8380) class="external" href="http://www.whitworth.manchester.ac.uk/collection/advsearch/objdispextra/index.html?irn=22540">http://www.whitworth.manchester.ac.uk/collection/advsearch/objdispextra/index.html?irn=22540 (tunic 2, t.8505) (accessed march 5, 2009). cite source "infant's tunics [object]," children youth history, item #210, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/210 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags 300-1000 ce, artifacts/objects, clothing, egypt, infancy, material culture, middle east/north africa related contentchildrens tunics [object]child’s sock [object]

content: primary source buffalo wheels [toy] annotation object made terracotta likely child's pull toy. approximately 11 centimeters high 16.5 centimeters long (4.3 x 6.5 inches), features functional wheels hole mouth string. buffalo shows traces stripes red paint, markings represent curly forelock. object dated archaic age (ca. 750-500 bce) magna graecia ("greater greece"), region southern part italian peninsula colonized greeks 8th century. toys presented greek children occasion festivals anniversaries, ritual function. dedicated dionysis, believed calm child remove evil spirits, especially toys made noise rattles whistles. source buffalo wheels, musée du louvre, paris campana collection, 1961 accession number cp 4699, department greek, etruscan roman antiquities, retrieved class="external" href="http://cartelen.louvre.fr/cartelen/visite?srv=car_not_frame&idnotice=7347"> http://cartelen.louvre.fr/cartelen/visite?srv=car_not_frame&idnotice=7347 (accessed march 9, 2009). color photograph marie-lan nguyen class="external" href="http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/file:toy_buffalo_louvre_cp4699.jpg"> http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/file:toy_buffalo_louvre_cp4699.jpg (accessed march 9, 2009). cite source "buffalo wheels [toy]," children youth history, item #211, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/211 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags 1000 bce-300 ce, artifacts/objects, europe, greece, material culture, play, toys

title: Children and Youth in History | American Indian Girls Playing with Dolls [Photograph]
content: primary source american indian girls playing dolls [photograph] annotation three photographs, taken near turn 20th century, american indian girls southwestern united states learning play mothers keepers home. first photograph, hopi girl arizona follows mother's example; wraps baby doll blanket carries back, contrast anglo girl holds doll arms. mescalero apache girls second photograph strapped baby dolls cradleboards, carry backs engaged labor, lean tree rock. tend doll-sized tepees wickiups, mescalero girls imitate mothers charge temporary homes could moved place place made spot followed seasonal supply food throughout southwestern borderlands. turn 20th century many white women missionaries social reformers regarded common indian ways mothering keeping house savage uncivilized. condemned use cradleboards regarded tepees wickiups, even hopi adobe homes, evidence indian women's savagery. believing transformation indian girls' methods raising children keeping house central assimilation indian people, many white women reformers promoted removal indian children families. instead, favored institutionalization distant boarding schools would taught middle-class anglo methods mothering, seen third photograph indian girls santa fe indian school new mexico. source nau.ph.99.54.166 (item 7165), image courtesy cline library, northern arizona university; ms 110 rg 81-38, new mexico state university library, archives special collections; students, ca. 1904, santa fe indian school, courtesy palace governors photo archives (nmhm/dca), negative #1035 courtesy palace governors (mnm/dca). cite source "american indian girls playing dolls [photograph]," children youth history, item #212, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/212 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated miriam forman-brunell tags 1750-1914, 1900-1945, american indian girls, arizona, dolls, hopi girl, north america, photographs

title: Children and Youth in History | Neolithic Baby Burial [Archeology]
content: primary source neolithic baby burial [archeology] annotation infant burial çatalhöyük neolithic settlement turkey occupied continuously 2,500 years, 8000 6400 bce. infant six months one year old, burial demonstrates great care. infant placed fetal position facing south rested reed mat basket. red ochre, mineral powder, used decorate body. two anklets made bone beads placed around feet, large bead bracelets made bone colored stone blue, black, white encircled infant's wrists. bead artifacts probably large worn baby alive, may burial offerings. archaeologists çatalhöyük preserved burial intact excavating underneath constructing box around source "mysteries çatalhöyük," science museum minnesota, ©2003, class="external" href="http://www.smm.org/catal/">http://www.smm.org/catal/ (accessed march 9, 2009) narrative charlie newman excavation diary entry, 16/05/99, cambridge university çatalhöyük archive class="external" href="http://www.smm.org/catal/mysteries/burial_practices/baby_comic/">http://www.smm.org/catal/mysteries/burial_practices/baby_comic/ (accessed march 9, 2009). cite source "neolithic baby burial [archeology]," children youth history, item #213, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/213 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags çatalhöyük, archaeology, artifacts/objects, beads, burial, funerary artifacts, infancy, material culture, middle east/north africa, mortality, turkey related contentsibling burial [archeology]earthenware mold swaddled child [object]

title: Children and Youth in History | Sibling Burial [Archeology]
content: primary source sibling burial [archeology] annotation two children whose skeletons shown photograph 10 years age, probably buried time. earlier burial baby found slightly lower level space archaeologists found burials floor house çatalhöyük, neolithic agricultural settlement turkey continuously occupied 8000 bce 6400 bce. many settlement's hundreds houses contained multiple burials. pits, sealed white plaster lids, lay beneath raised platforms people slept worked, living close ancestors. houses ritually closed collapsed upon death important family member. according analysis skeletons found çatalhöyük, life expectancy averaged 34 years men 28 years women. source berkeley archaeologists çatalhöyük (bach) catalhoyuk research project, remixing catalhoyuk class="external" href="http://okapi.dreamhosters.com/remixing/mainpage.html"> http://okapi.dreamhosters.com/remixing/mainpage.html (accessed march 9, 2009). image class="external" href="http://128.32.93.100/netpub/server.np?find&catalog=catalog&template=detail.np&field=itemid&op=matches&value=429&site=life"> http://128.32.93.100/netpub/server.np?find&catalog=catalog&template=detail.np&field=itemid&op=matches&value=429&site=life (accessed march 9, 2009). cite source "sibling burial [archeology]," children youth history, item #214, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/214 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags çatalhöyük, archaeology, infancy, material culture, middle east/north africa, beginnings human society, turkey related contentearthenware mold swaddled child [object]neolithic baby burial [archeology]

title: Children and Youth in History | Earthenware Mold of a Swaddled Child [Object]
content: primary source earthenware mold swaddled child [object] annotation earthenware mold casting figurine infant found tangyangu china, likely dated 960 1279 ce, song dynasty. mold measures 3.2 inches long, belongs collection molds depicting men, women, animals. molds likely used make tomb figurines placed dead burial, ritual practice becoming less widespread song dynasty. mold infant figurine shows widespread practice swaddling, tightly binding newborn infants strips cloth. cultures western eastern asia, believed practice necessary, helped form child's body preventing much movement. practice swaddling abandoned modern times, recent pediatric article noted may help newborns sleep, calm heartbeat, prevent sudden infant death. source (c) trustees british museum, london, object an273723001, acquisition date 1933 class="external" href="http://www.britishmuseum.org/research/search_the_collection_database/search_object_image.aspx?objectid=259843&partid=1&searchtext=child&orig=%2fresearch%2fsearch_the_collection_database.aspx&numpages=10¤tpage=7&asset_id=273723">http://www.britishmuseum.org/research/search_the_collection_database/search_object_image.aspx?objectid=259843&partid=1&searchtext=child&orig=%2fresearch%2fsearch_the_collection_database.aspx&numpages=10¤tpage=7&asset_id=273723 (accessed march 9, 2009). cite source "earthenware mold swaddled child [object]," children youth history, item #215, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/215 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags 1000-1500 ce, 300-1000 ce, archaeology, artifacts/objects, china, cultural practices, east asia, infancy, material culture, medical practices, song dynasty, swaddling related contentneolithic baby burial [archeology]sibling burial [archeology]

title: Children and Youth in History | Emile [Literary Excerpt]
content: primary source emile [literary excerpt] annotation french philosopher jean-jacques rousseau wrote philosophical treatise emile, education 1762. imagines situation young tutor devotes 20 years raising single child. process, rousseau lays many fundamental beliefs nature humans relationship society. source rousseau, jean-jacques. emile. new york: e. p. dutton, 1969. quoted barbara kaye greenleaf. children ages: history childhood (new york: mcgraw-hill, 1978), 64, 67, 755. primary source text nature wants children children men. deliberately pervert order, shall get premature fruits neither ripe well-flavored, soon decay. childhood ways seeing, thinking, feeling peculiar nothing foolish substitute ways child's worth increases years. personal value must added cost care bestowed upon loss life joined sentiment death. therefore future must think watching conservation; ills childhood must armed even gets value life increases child reaches age useful, crazy spare suffering infancy multiply pain reaches age reason? child's first sentiment love second, derives first, love around present state weakness aware people help attention receives first affection nurse governess mere habit. seeks needs feels good like consciousness benevolence. needs long time understand useful want useful begins love nature exercises children continually, hardens temperament kinds difficulties, teaches early meaning pain sorrow. teething gives fevers, sharp colics bring convulsions, long coughing suffocates worms torment plethora corrupts blood, various leavens ferment cause dangerous eruptions. almost first age sickness danger: one half children born die eighth year. tests passed, infant gained strength, soon make use life principle becomes secure. cite source "emile [literary excerpt]," children youth history, item #216, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/216 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated sharon cohen tags emile, 1750-1914, europe, france, jean-jacques rousseau, literary sources

title: Children and Youth in History | Madonna and Child, 1295–1300 [Painting]
content: primary source madonna child, 1295–1300 [painting] annotation panel painting represents innovation history western european painting moment religious iconography: depiction virgin mary infant jesus byzantine icons traditionally symbolic. past, figures humanized, represented qualities divine beings episodes sacred history. painting late 13th century italian painter duccio di buoninsega, contrast, expresses emotions love tenderness mother child. infant jesus returns madonna's placid intense gaze. painter depicted jesus life-like, natural pose toes touching mother's hand, one foot slightly raised reaches play folds mother's veil, almost touching cheek. artist retained many features iconographic painting, including use gold background, basic composition two figures, added humanizing element. although figure christ child panel playful previous artistic depictions, physique still middle-aged man baby. typical representation children also donning adult clothing led widespread assumption childhood separate stage life exist. however, recent research draws upon wide range primary sources—iconography, archaeology, letters, romances, pedagogical texts, miracle accounts, law codes—reveals children regarded vulnerable need special protection. examinations everyday lives children demonstrate, example, orphans protected abuse medieval laws sick children received different medical treatments adults. source madonna child, ca. 1295–1300, duccio di buoninsegna (italian, active 1278, died 1318) tempera gold wood, original engaged frame; title: Children and Youth in History | Mud Crocodile [Toy]

content: primary source indus valley climbing monkey toy [object] annotation monkey figurine shown two views mounted wooden platform display, apparently designed ability slide string thin rod appear climb jump. figurine hand-modeled terracotta features fully rounded body complicated, lifelike pose realistic facial expression, ears, incised lines representing fur. monkey excavated indus valley archaeological site called mohenjo-daro, dated ca. 2500 b.c.e. excavations sites indus valley included many figurines made terracotta, clearly entertainment, including whistles shaped like birds, small animals including bulls moveable heads horns, toy carts. archaeologists classified monkey figurine toy. figurine 5.5 centimeters (2.1 inches) high. source climbing monkey hand-modeled, indus valley, delhi national museum; set 61: indian terracottas indian earth 6103; copyright acsaa slide (c)aaaum. cite source "indus valley climbing monkey toy [object]," children youth history, item #312, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/312 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotated susan douglass tags 4000-1000 bce, ancient, artifacts/objects, entertainment, india, indus valley, material culture, play, south/southeast asia, toys

title: Children and Youth in History | Colonial Childhoods Oral History Project [Oral History]
content: primary source colonial childhoods oral history project [oral history] alt 1-01 alice cross.mp3 annotation colonial childhoods oral history project (ccohp) comprises recorded interviews 165 new zealanders, male female, maori pakeha, majority born 1903. interviews focus period individual’s 15th birthday. participants discussed wide range topics relating culture childhood, including home life, sibling influences, school church activities, leisure, chores, friends, hobbies, values, clothes, parents, favorite foods, special occasions. also responded questions concerning sexuality, drink, violence. interviews generally lasted two three hours always preceded preliminary meeting interviewer ethical issues covered permission forms processed. interviewees felt comfortable talking every topic, ccohp interviewers sensitive respecting preferences. discuss sexuality frank forthright. diversity childhood experience one obvious finding project, pakeha patterns apparent accumulated evidence. wearing "hand-me-down" clothes commonplace, particularly larger families, sharing beds bedrooms. informants felt well-instructed sexuality, topic parents generally reluctant discuss. grew farms tended reach conclusions observation animal behavior. town-dwellers relied heavily hearsay case one boy, straight talking local scoutmaster. era pregnancy outside marriage strongly condemned. one male interviewee alluded tales experimentation classmate. source female interviewee 35 (born 1897). interview colonial childhoods oral history project (ccohp), new zealand, february 9, 1989, tape 1. author's possession. annotated jeanine graham. primary source text brother born 12 night born, mother "would like go round stay mrs andrews?" stayed night came home morning. mrs andrews "oh, go home went home. wasn't far living petone. got side saw beautiful baby basket, chair, dining room saw somebody rushing round starched apron cap head thought, "well, "who's basket?" title: Children and Youth in History | "I Didn't Raise My Boy to Be a Soldier" [Song]
content: primary source didn't raise boy soldier" [song] annotation 1915, americans began debating need military economic preparations war. strong opposition "preparedness" came isolationists, socialists, pacifists, many protestant ministers, german americans, irish americans hostile britain). one hit songs 1915, didn't raise boy soldier," lyricist alfred bryan composer al piantadosi, captured widespread american skepticism joining european war. meanwhile, interventionists militarists like former president theodore roosevelt beat drums preparedness. roosevelt’s retort popularity antiwar song accompanied tune didn't raise girl mother." suggested place women opposed war china—or preference harem—and united states." source al pianadosi alfred bryan, didn't raise boy soldier." recording: edison collection, library congress. primary source text ten million soldiers war gone, may never return ten million mothers' hearts must break, ones died vain. head bowed sorrowin lonely years, heard mother murmur thro' tears: chorus: didn't raise boy soldier, brought pride joy, dares put musket shoulder, shoot mother's darling boy? let nations arbitrate future troubles, time lay sword gun away, there'd war today, mothers would say, didn’t raise boy soldier. (chorus) victory cheer mother’s heart, looks blighted home? victory bring back, cared call let mother answer year remember boy belongs (chorus) cite source didn't raise boy soldier" [song]," children youth history, item #396, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/396 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags didn't raise boy soldier", 1900-1945, al piantadosi, alfred bryan, music, war

title: Children and Youth in History | Charlotte Temple: A Tale of Truth (1791) [Literary Excerpt]
content: primary source charlotte temple: tale truth (1791) [literary excerpt] annotation charlotte temple: tale truth, published 1791, first american bestseller. author, susanna haswell rowson, born england circa 1762, died 1824 massachusetts, spent life. charlotte temple tells story young english girl lured away school army officer, montraville. board ship posting revolutionary-era new york, montraville seduces charlotte. new york, montraville gradually abandons "ruined" charlotte downward spiral remorse, illness, poverty, birth child, dies. seduction novels popular 18th century, widely read charlotte temple went 200 editions. rowson, despite childhood daughter english revenue officer became committed republican, used novel protest sexual double standard ruined lives women like charlotte. source susanna haswell rowson, charlotte temple: tale truth, ed. francis w. halsey (philadelphia: matthew carey, 1794; repr., new york: funk wagnalls co., 1905), vol.2, pp.3–10. primary source text chapter 18 reflections indeed fallen low," charlotte, pitied? voice approbation meet ear? shall never possess friend, whose face wear smile joy whenever approach? alas! thoughtless, dreadfully imprudent know painful endure, sneer contempt, glance compassion, depicted various countenances sex: equally humiliating. ah! dear parents, could see child affections, daughter dearly loved, poor solitary without society, wearing heavy hours deep regret anguish heart, kind friend sex unbosom griefs, beloved mother, woman character appear company, low charlotte fallen, cannot associate infamy." painful reflections occupied mind charlotte. montraville placed small house miles new-york: gave one female attendant, supplied money wanted; business pleasure entirely occupied time, little devote woman, brought connections, robbed innocence. sometimes, indeed, would steal close evening, pass hours much attached sorrows forgotten blest society: would enjoy walk moonlight, sit little arbour bottom garden, play harp, accompanying plaintive, harmonious voice. often, often, promise renew visits, forgetful promise, leave mourn disappointment. painful hours expectation would pass! would sit window looked toward field used cross, counting minutes, straining eyes catch first glimpse person, till blinded tears disappointment, would lean head hands, give free vent sorrows: catching new hope, would renew watchful position, till shades evening enveloped every object dusky cloud: would renew complaints, heart bursting disappointed love wounded sensibility, retire bed remorse strewed thorns, court vain comforter weary nature seldom visits unhappy) come steep senses oblivion. form adequate idea sorrow preyed upon mind charlotte? wife, whose breast glows affection husband, return meets indifference, faintly conceive anguish. dreadfully painful situation woman, many comforts poor charlotte deprived. duteous, faithful wife, though treated indifference, one solid pleasure within bosom, reflect deserved neglect—that ever fulfilled duties station strictest exactness; may hope, constant assiduity unremitted attention, recall wanderer, doubly happy returning affection; knows cannot leave unite another: cannot cast poverty contempt. looks around sees smile friendly welcome, tear affectionate consolation, face every person favours esteem; circumstances gathers comfort: poor girl thoughtless passion led astray, parting honour, forfeited esteem man sacrificed every thing dear valuable life, feels indifference fruit folly, laments want power recall lost affection; knows tie honour, man guilty seduction, feeble: may leave moment shame want; may marry forsake ever; redress, friendly, soothing companion pour wounded mind balm consolation, benevolent hand lead back path rectitude; disgraced friends, forfeited good opinion world, undone feels poor solitary midst surrounding multitudes; shame bows earth, remorse tears distracted mind, guilt, poverty, disease close dreadful scene: sinks unnoticed oblivion. finger contempt may point passing daughter youthful mirth, humble bed lies frail sister mortality; unbounded gaiety heart, exult unblemished fame, triumph silent ashes dead? oh heart sensibility, stop, thus address unhappy victim folly— "thou had'st thy faults, sure thy sufferings expiated thy errors brought thee early grave; thou wert fellow-creature—thou hast unhappy—then errors forgotten." stoops pluck noxious weed sod, tear fall, consecrate spot charity. ever honoured sacred drop humanity; angel mercy shall record source, soul whence sprang shall immortal. dear madam, contract brow frown disapprobation. mean extenuate faults unhappy women fall victims guilt folly; surely, reflect many errors subject many secret faults lie hid recesses hearts, blush brought open day yet faults require lenity pity benevolent judge, awful would prospect futurity) say, dear madam, consider surely may pity faults others. believe many unfortunate female, strayed thorny paths vice, would gladly return virtue, generous friend endeavour raise re-assure alas! cannot say; world would deride scoff. let tell madam, 'tis unfeeling world, deserve half blessings bountiful providence showers upon oh, thou benevolent giver good! shall erring mortals dare look thy mercy great day retribution, uncharitably refuse overlook errors, alleviate miseries, fellow-creatures! cite source "charlotte temple: tale truth (1791) [literary excerpt]," children youth history, item #399, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/399 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1750-1914, charlotte temple: tale truth, literary sources, north america, susanna haswell rowson

title: Children and Youth in History | A Narrative of the Life of Mrs. Mary Jemison [Literary Excerpt]
content: primary source narrative life mrs. mary jemison [literary excerpt] annotation 1753, 15 year old mary jemison captured indians along pennsylvania frontier seven years' war french, english, indian peoples north america. adopted incorporated senecas, familiar practice among iroquois indian peoples seeking replace lost sibling spouse. mary married raised family decades american revolution; many captives, adopted integrated indian community, refused opportunity return home, finding life indian society rewarding. 1823 mary jemison related life story james seaver, doctor lived near home western new york. seaver's story white woman genessee," became known, sold 100,000 copies 1824. source james e. seaver, narrative life mrs. mary jemison (canadaigua, n.y.: j.d. bemis, 1824), 43–51. primary source text made fast shore, squaws left canoe went wigwam house town, returned suit indian clothing, new, clean nice. clothes, though whole good taken, torn pieces, almost naked. first undressed threw rags river; washed clean dressed new suit brought, complete indian style; led home seated center wigwam. situation hut minutes, squaws town came see soon surrounded immediately set dismal howling, crying bitterly, wringing hands agonies grief deceased relative. tears flowed freely, exhibited signs real mourning. commencement scene, one number began, voice somewhat speaking singing, recite words following purport, continued recitation till ceremony ended; company time varying appearance countenances, gestures tune voice, correspond sentiments expressed leader: "oh brother! alas! dead—he gone; never return! friendless died field slain, bones yet lying un-buried! oh, mourn sad fate? tears dropped around oh, tears sisters fell prune, arm needed keep us danger! alas! gone! left us sorrow, loss bewail: oh spirit? spirit went naked, hungry wanders, thirsty wounded groans return! oh helpless wretched, brother gone! blanket food nourish warm candles light weapons war:—oh, none comforts well remember deeds! deer could take chase! panther shrunk back sight strength! enemies fell feet! brave courageous war! fawn harmless: friendship ardent: temper gentle: pity great! oh! friend, companion dead! brother, brother, alas! gone! grieve loss? strength warrior, undaunted left us, fight side chiefs! war-whoop shrill! rifle well aimed laid enemies low: tomahawk drank blood: knife flayed scalps yet covered gore! mourn? though fell field slain, glory fell, spirit went land fathers war! mourn? transports joy received fed clothed welcomed oh friends, happy; dry tears! spirit seen distress, sent us helper pleasure greet. dickewamis come: let us receive joy! handsome pleasant! oh! sister, gladly welcome place brother stands tribe. care guard trouble; may happy till spirit shall leave us." course ceremony, mourning became serene—joy sparkled countenances, seemed rejoice long-lost child. made welcome amongst sister two squaws mentioned, called dickewamis; interpreted, signifies pretty girl, handsome girl, pleasant, good thing. name ever since called indians. afterwards learned ceremony time passed adoption. two squaws lost brother washington's war, sometime year consequence death went fort pitt, day arrived order receive prisoner oil enemy's scalp, supply loss. custom indians, one number slain taken prisoner battle, give nearest relative dead absent, prisoner, chanced take one, give scalp enemy. return indians conquest, always announced peculiar shoutings, demonstrations joy, exhibition trophy victory, mourners come forward make claims. receive prisoner, option either satiate vengeance taking life cruel manner conceive receive adopt family, place lost. prisoners taken battle carried encampment town indians, given bereaved families, till number made good. unless mourners received news bereavement, operation paroxysm grief, anger revenge; unless prisoner old, sickly, homely, generally save treat kindly. mental wound fresh, loss great deem irreparable, prisoner prisoners meet approbation, torture, let ever cruel, seems sufficient make satisfaction. family, national, sacrifices amongst indians, given indelible stamp barbarians, identified character idea generally formed unfeeling ferocity, abandoned cruelty. happy lot accepted adoption: time ceremony received two squaws, supply tile place mother family; ever considered treated real sister, though horn mother. adoption, sat motionless, nearly terrified death appearance actions company, expecting every moment feel vengeance, suffer death spot. however, happily disappointed, close ceremony company retired, sisters went employing every means consolation comfort. settled provided home, employed nursing children, light work house. occasionally sent indian hunters, went short distance, help carry game. situation easy; particular hardships endure. still, recollection parents, brothers sisters, home, captivity, destroyed happiness, made constantly solitary, lonesome gloomy. sisters would allow speak english hearing; remembering charge dear mother gave time left whenever chanced alone made business repeating prayer, catechism, something learned order might forget language. practising way retained till t came genesee flats, soon became acquainted english people almost daily habit conversing. sisters diligent teaching language; great satisfaction soon learned could understand readily, speak fluently. fortunate falling hands; kind good natured women; peaceable mild dispositions; temperate decent habits, tender gentle toward great reason respect though dead great number years. town lived pleasantly situated ohio, mouth shenanjee: land produced good corn; woods furnished plenty game, waters abounded fish. another river emptied ohio, directly opposite mouth shenanjee. spent summer place, planted, hoed, harvested large crop corn, excellent quality. time corn harvest, fort pitt taken french english. corn harvested, indians took horses canoes, proceeded ohio, occasionally stopping hunt days, till arrived mouth sciota river; established winter quarters, continued hunting till ensuing spring, adjacent wilderness. place went children assist hunters bring game. forests sciota well stocked elk, deer, large animals; marshes contained large numbers beaver, muskrat, etc., made excellent hunting indians; depended, meat, upon success taking elk deer; ammunition clothing, upon beaver, muskrat, furs could take addition peltry. season hunting passed, returned spring mouth river shenanjee houses fields left fall planted corn, squashes, beans, fields occupied preceding summer. planting time, indians went fort pitt, make peace british, took landed opposite side river fort, encamped night. early next morning indians took fort see white people heart bounded liberated indians restored friends country. white people surprised see indians, enduring hardships savage life, early age, delicate constitution t appeared possess. asked name; taken—and appeared much interested behalf. continuing inquiries, sisters became alarmed, believing taken hurried canoe recrossed river—took bread fire fled without stopping, till arrived river shenanjee. great fear losing given treaty, never stopped rowing till got home. shortly left shore opposite fort, informed one indian brothers, white people came take back; alter considerable inquiry, made diligent search find hid, returned heavy hearts. although indians something year, become considerably habituated mode living, attached sisters, sight white people could speak english inspired unspeakable anxiety go home share blessings civilization. sudden departure escape seemed like second captivity, long time brooded thoughts miserable situation almost much sorrow dejection t done first sufferings. time, destroyer every affection, wore away unpleasant feelings, became contented cite source narrative life mrs. mary jemison [literary excerpt]," children youth history, item #400, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/400 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1750-1914, james seaver, literary sources, mary jemison, native americans, north america, personal accounts, seven years' war

content: primary source bar miztvah [photograph] three boys bar mitzvah.jpg annotation three boys photograph belong group israeli boys came together western wall jerusalem 2008 mass bar mitzvah celebration. orthodox social service organization colel chabad arranged celebration orphans needy families. several prominent israeli rabbis participated celebration important jewish rite passage adulthood. "bar mitzvah" literally means "son commandment." rite passage marks time, age 13 boys, children obligated observe commandments jewish law. technically, ceremony needed, boy automatically passes milestone upon becoming 13 years old. recent times, bar mitzvah become popular widespread individual family observance. shabbat (congregational prayer) services saturday shortly child's 13th birthday, celebrant called torah recite blessing weekly reading. preparation day, young boy spends time learning torah portion prayers. community service often part preparatory period bar mitzvah. speech congregation occasion traditionally begins phrase, "today man. father also recites blessing. source photograph copyright chabad.org article class="external" href="http://www.chabad.org/news/article_cdo/aid/933026/jewish/mass-bar-mitzvah-at-western-wall.htm">http://www.chabad.org/news/article_cdo/aid/933026/jewish/mass-bar-mitzvah-at-western-wall.htm (accessed march 30, 2010). annotated susan douglass. cite source "bar miztvah [photograph]," children youth history, item #436, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/436 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags boy, boys, israel, jerusalem, judaism, middle east/north africa, photographs, religion, rites passage, rituals, world since 1945, youth

title: Children and Youth in History | Advice of an Aztec Father to His Sons [Document]

title: Children and Youth in History | Benjamín Montes with Bourgan, Funge, and Company [Labor Contract]
content: primary source benjamín montes bourgan, funge, company [labor contract] annotation since 1810, social critics buenos aires long concerned young people lower classes—especially young men—exercising greater independence within home. decline parental authority, alarmed sight growing numbers young people potential source disorder, looked state solutions. result, police granted broad authority place youngsters jobs would keep streets supply businesses homes badly needed laborers. 1841, benjamín montes, minor, signed contract bourgan, funge, company, hat factory buenos aires, work three years apprentice. first glance, terms contract seem harsh. limited montes's mobility, governed behavior factory, work probably grueling. however, type arrangement also illustrative weakening parental authority favor state authority. montes's mother, juana maría olivera, clearly transferred power son company (mothers could sign contracts male heads households unavailable). kinds jobs provided greater opportunities young people, like benjamín, gain enough income make life reached adulthood. source archivo general de la nación, x-31-9-5, policía, 1823–50. reprinted szuchman, mark d. order, family, community buenos aires, 1810-1860. stanford, ca: stanford university press, 1988. annotated jesse hingson. primary source text long live federation! content: primary source scouts' war dance": sir robert baden powell's adaptation zulu chant, c1910s [chant] annotation like much public turn-of-the-century britain, baden powell fascinated "primitive" cultures. although claimed expert knowledge africa service colonial wars, baden powell hardly authority zulu customs. matter, metropolitan britons almost entirely ignorant african institutions. nevertheless, fascinated romanticized depictions new colonial subjects popular press, juvenile literature, memoirs colonial war heroes. confident cultural superiority, british came believe african peoples like zulu preserved simpler, savage, nobler qualities seemed disappearing modern industrial society. baden powell built popular support scout movement tapping sentiments. claimed based scout ranks zulu age grades used ndebele "war horn" call scouts order. content: teaching module educational reform japan (19th c.) brian platt, george mason university preamble fundamental code education, 1872 [government document]annotationthe following paragraphs came beginning 109-article plan, promulgated 1872, establish national school system direction new meiji government. ambitious plan divided country eight university districts, divided 32 middle-school districts. plan drew upon close examination educational systems west—the u.s. france, particular—and reflected desire part meiji government make schooling compulsory centralized. several decades would pass goal fully realized. nonetheless, important note new government prioritized inception—at time still unstable financially strapped—the goal standardized, compulsory, centralized schooling.sourcepreamble fundamental code education, 1872. yoshida, kumaji. title: The Dolben's Act of 1788 [Government Document] - Children and Youth in History | Children in the Slave Trade
content: teaching module children slave trade colleen vasconcellos, university west georgia dolben's act 1788 [government document]annotationthe dolben's act 1788 proposed noted abolitionist sir william dolben english parliament. meant restrict slave trade, actually adverse effect children. act mandated two fifths ship's cargo children, also limited number african men 1 male per ship ton. restrictions threatening slave supply, planter demand began change response. since act define 'child,' children ages 12 18 entered trade. furthermore, act sparked important debate benefits breeding slaves rather buying consequently, act somewhat responsible increased number girls children trade.sourcedonnan, elizabeth. documents illustrative slave trade america. volume 2. new york: octagon books, 1965, 583-87. annotated colleen vasconcellos.primary source textan act regulate, limited time, shipping carrying slaves british vessels coast africa. whereas expedient regulate shipping carrying slaves british vessels coast africa; therefore enacted. shall lawful master, person taking charge command british ship vessel whatever, shall clear port kingdom first day august one thousand seven hundred eighty eight, board, one time, convey, carry, bring, transport slaves coast africa parts beyond sea, ship vessel, greater number proportion five slaves every three tons burthen ship vessel, burthen ship vessel, far ship vessel shall exceed two hundred one tons; moreover, one slave every additional ton ship vessel, burthen two hundred one tons, male slaves shall exceed four feet four inches height, greater number proportion one male slave every one ton burthen ship vessel, far ship vessel shall exceed two hundred one tons, three male slaves shall exceed height four feet four inches) every additional five tons ship vessel, burthen two hundred one tons. master, person taking charge command ship vessel, shall act contrary hereto, master, person aforesaid, shall forfeit pay sum thirty pounds lawful money great britain, every slave exceeding number proportions herein-before limited. ii. provided always, shall ship vessel, two fifth parts slaves shall children, shall exceed four feet four inches height, every five children aforesaid proportion two fifths) shall deemed taken equal four slaves within true intent meaning act. viii. person hindering process ascertaining number negroes vessel fined £100. xi. enacted authority aforesaid. shall lawful person become master, take command charge ship vessel time shall clear port great britain, purchasing carrying slaves coast africa, unless master, person taking charge command ship vessel, shall already served capacity one voyage, shall served chief mate surgeon whole two voyages, either chief mate, three voyages, purchasing carrying slaves coast africa; pain master, person taking charge command ship vessel, also owner owners, shall hire employ person, shall, every offence respectively, forfeit pay sum fifty pounds. xiv. enacted authority aforesaid…that shall died proportion two slaves hundred, time arrival ship vessel coast africa, time arrival port discharge islands west indies, belonging dominion majesty, case, collector principal officer aforesaid shall, hereby authorised required make certificates, specifying number slaves appear taken board ship vessel, number died within period mentioned; one certificates shall delivered master, surgeon ship vessel; production certificates, commissioners majesty's customs england scotland respectively shall, hereby authorised required direct sum one hundred pounds paid master, sum fifty pounds paid surgeon ship vessel, money shall hands receiver general customs england scotland respectively; shall made appear collector, principal officer aforesaid, shall died proportion three slaves hundred, time arrival ship vessel coast africa, time arrival port discharge west india islands, case collector principal officer aforesaid shall, hereby authorised required make like certificates, deliver one master, surgeon ship vessel; commissioners customs england scotland respectively shall, hereby authorised required, production certificates, direct sum fifty pounds paid master, sum twenty five pounds paid surgeon ship vessel. xx. enacted, act shall continue force till first day august one thousand seven hundred eighty-nine, longer, except purpose trying suing person consequence offence offences committed breach violation act.' cite source colleen vasconcellos, "children slave trade," children youth history, item #141, enemy large, suppose consisting six thousand men. leader called baukurre. destroying old prince, decamped immediately marched towards sea, lying west, taking women prisoners. march, scouting party detached main army. leader party made waiter, carry gun, etc. a-scouting, came across herd fat cattle consisting thirty number. set upon immediately wrested keepers, afterwards converted food army. enemy remarkable success destroying country wherever went. far penetrated laid habitations waste captured people. distance brought four hundred miles. march hard tasks imposed must perform pain punishment. obliged carry head large flat stone used grinding corn, weighing, suppose, much twenty-five pounds; besides victuals, mat cooking utensils. though pretty large stout age, yet burdens grievous six years half old. come place called malagasco. entered place, could see least appearance either house inhabitants, stricter search found instead houses ground dens sides hillocks, contiguous ponds streams water. perceived hid suppose usually occasions. order compel surrender, enemy contrived smoke faggots. put entrance caves set fire. engaged business, great surprise desperately wounded arrows fell mystery soon found perceived enemy discharged arrows holes top dens directly air. weight brought back, point downwards, enemies heads, whilst smoking inhabitants points arrows poisoned, enemy antidote instantly applied wounded part. smoke last obliged people give came caves, first spatting palms hands together, immediately extended arms, crossed wrists, ready bound pinioned. judge dens mentioned extended eight feet horizontally earth, six feet height, many wide. arched overhead lined earth, clay kind made surface walls firm smooth. invaders pinioned prisoners ages sexes indiscriminately, took flocks effects, moved way towards sea. march, prisoners treated clemency, account submissive humble. come next tribe, enemy laid siege immediately took men, women, children, flocks, valuable effects. went next district, contiguous sea, called africa, anamaboo. enemies' provisions almost spent, well strength. inhabitants, knowing conduct pursued, present intentions, improved favorable opportunity, attacked hem, took enemy, prisoners, flocks effects. liken second time. us put castle kept market. certain time, prisoners put board canoe, master, rowed away vessel belonging rhode land, commanded captain collingwood, mate, thomas mumford. going vessel, master told us appear best possible advantage sale. bought board one robertson mumford, steward vessel, four gallons rum piece calico, called venture account purchased private venture. thus came name. slaves bought vessel's cargo two hundred twenty. vessel sailed rhode island, arrived comfortable passage. completed eighth year. cite source colleen vasconcellos, "children slave trade," children youth history, item #141, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/141 (accessed august 10, us attempted, vain, run away, pistols cut-lasses soon introduced, threatening, offered stir lie dead spot. one pretended friendly rest, would speak lord get us clear, desired follow immediately divided different parties, drove soon led way knew, towards evening, came sight town, told us great man lived pretended late go see night. next morning came three men, whose language differed spoke watched us night, pretended friend great man, others, gone away. asked keepers men saying answered, asking us together, go feast day, must put seeing great man till little thinking doom nigh, villains meant feast us prey. went half day’s journey, came great multitude people, different music playing; day got merry music, dancing singing. towards evening, persuaded could get back great man lived till next day; bedtime came, separated different houses different people. next morning came, asked men brought rest companions; told gone sea side bring home rum, guns powder, companions gone gone fields something gave strong suspicion treachery case, began think hopes returning home soon became uneasy, knowing refused eat drink whole days together, till man house told would power get back uncle; eat little fruit thoughts sought would missing home five six days. enquired every day men come back, rest companions could get answer satisfaction kept six days man’s house, evening another man came talked good heard one say must go, sooner better; man came told knew relations agimaque, must set to-morrow morning, would convey accordingly, set next day, travelled till dark, came place supper slept. carried large bag gold dust, buy goods sea side take agimaque. next day travelled evening came town, saw several white people, made afraid would eat according notion children inland parts country. made rest uneasy night, next morning victuals brought, desiring eat make haste, guide kid-napper told go castle company going told get goods. ordered horrors soon saw felt, cannot well described; saw many miserable countrymen chained two two, hand-cuffed, hands tied behind. conducted along guard, arrived castle, asked guide brought told learn ways brow- sow, white faced people. saw
