title: Introduction - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city introductionchildren youth early modern england (1500-1800) subject many diseases physical hardships. great epidemic diseases bubonic plague smallpox, common illnesses measles influenza still afflict children today, sickness put children youth great risk. knowledge bacteria antibiotics, surgery performed without anesthesia even hand washing, remedies childhood illnesses beyond nourishing diet keeping patient warm. even surviving illness could permanent consequences, example, scarlet fever left many children blind deaf, measles could cause severe scarring facial bone loss. one measurement health early modern england revealed statistics number deaths kept church parishes. records historians gleaned infant mortality (death first year life) approximately 140 1000 live births. average mother 7-8 live births 15 years. unidentifiable fevers, following list diseases, killed perhaps 30% england's children age 15 bloody flux (dysentery), scarlatina (scarlet fever), whooping cough, influenza, smallpox, pneumonia. death disease higher urban rural areas. early modern cities widely, often rightly, regarded deadly environments. contained large concentrations population often poorly fed housed. "crowd diseases" typhus, smallpox, tuberculosis prospered, bubonic plague epidemics periodically swept dense urban populations. 1563, 1603, 1625 1665, one fifth population london died plague outbreaks. 1665, one deadliest years, 80,000 people died capital city. number, historians estimate least 45,000 victims age 15. besides diseases, accidents common sources sickness, disability death children youth. surveys coroners's inquests, drowning wells bathtubs, reported accidental death children age 5. accidents also reported connected work children engaged beginning around age 8. children cracked skulls fetching water, trampled horses ploughing, dropped injured care siblings. boys, unless noblest families, expected serve apprenticeship. often placed dangerous crafts tanning, blacksmithing, serving ships, chemical poisonings, fires, war injuries frequent occurrences. also accounts diaries period youthful pranks leading injury, example, hiding gunpowder candles blew lit. throughout period primary place sick children youth cared home, principal healers women mothers, daughters, wives, servants. powder burn remedies —applying mixture poultry fat dung—were commonly included home receit (remedy/recipe) books kept mistress household. women developed considerable professional knowledge rise printing press 1500 publication books hands physicians. herbal chemical medicines described suitable young family receit books, dried dill honey cough, iron filings beer paleness skin. children rarely treated small expensive elite university-trained physicians adult patients turned prognosis cure. remedies also considered drastic children largely consisted rectal purging (laxatives), bloodletting (cutting vein open lancet), forced vomiting (emetics). treatments based ancient greek medical theory body composed four substances, humors, created digestion food. four humors choler yellow bile, phlegm mucus, black bile, blood, properties hot/cold dry/wet. humors balanced neither strong weak healthy. hot wet humor blood hot dry humor yellow bile believed naturally stronger young. occasionally humors weakened released body form sweat, tears, urine, feces, even sneezing, physicians would give children emetics make vomit let blood "cupping." heated glass, bone, brass cups would placed upon skin scratched scarified knife. blood would flow gently wounds due creation vacuum heated cup. worried parents consulted surgeons, trained apprenticeship, broken limbs, ruptures, bladder stone. latter caused early modern diet, rich gravel. boys often operated stone surgeons period mortality rate 30%. operation called lithotomy took three five minutes perform. anesthesia used, instead surgeons relied child fainting pain extraction stone. often, parents turned title: Introduction - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city introductionchildren youth early modern england (1500-1800) subject many diseases physical hardships. great epidemic diseases bubonic plague smallpox, common illnesses measles influenza still afflict children today, sickness put children youth great risk. knowledge bacteria antibiotics, surgery performed without anesthesia even hand washing, remedies childhood illnesses beyond nourishing diet keeping patient warm. even surviving illness could permanent consequences, example, scarlet fever left many children blind deaf, measles could cause severe scarring facial bone loss. one measurement health early modern england revealed statistics number deaths kept church parishes. records historians gleaned infant mortality (death first year life) approximately 140 1000 live births. average mother 7-8 live births 15 years. unidentifiable fevers, following list diseases, killed perhaps 30% england's children age 15 bloody flux (dysentery), scarlatina (scarlet fever), whooping cough, influenza, smallpox, pneumonia. death disease higher urban rural areas. early modern cities widely, often rightly, regarded deadly environments. contained large concentrations population often poorly fed housed. "crowd diseases" typhus, smallpox, tuberculosis prospered, bubonic plague epidemics periodically swept dense urban populations. 1563, 1603, 1625 1665, one fifth population london died plague outbreaks. 1665, one deadliest years, 80,000 people died capital city. number, historians estimate least 45,000 victims age 15. besides diseases, accidents common sources sickness, disability death children youth. surveys coroners's inquests, drowning wells bathtubs, reported accidental death children age 5. accidents also reported connected work children engaged beginning around age 8. children cracked skulls fetching water, trampled horses ploughing, dropped injured care siblings. boys, unless noblest families, expected serve apprenticeship. often placed dangerous crafts tanning, blacksmithing, serving ships, chemical poisonings, fires, war injuries frequent occurrences. also accounts diaries period youthful pranks leading injury, example, hiding gunpowder candles blew lit. throughout period primary place sick children youth cared home, principal healers women mothers, daughters, wives, servants. powder burn remedies —applying mixture poultry fat dung—were commonly included home receit (remedy/recipe) books kept mistress household. women developed considerable professional knowledge rise printing press 1500 publication books hands physicians. herbal chemical medicines described suitable young family receit books, dried dill honey cough, iron filings beer paleness skin. children rarely treated small expensive elite university-trained physicians adult patients turned prognosis cure. remedies also considered drastic children largely consisted rectal purging (laxatives), bloodletting (cutting vein open lancet), forced vomiting (emetics). treatments based ancient greek medical theory body composed four substances, humors, created digestion food. four humors choler yellow bile, phlegm mucus, black bile, blood, properties hot/cold dry/wet. humors balanced neither strong weak healthy. hot wet humor blood hot dry humor yellow bile believed naturally stronger young. occasionally humors weakened released body form sweat, tears, urine, feces, even sneezing, physicians would give children emetics make vomit let blood "cupping." heated glass, bone, brass cups would placed upon skin scratched scarified knife. blood would flow gently wounds due creation vacuum heated cup. worried parents consulted surgeons, trained apprenticeship, broken limbs, ruptures, bladder stone. latter caused early modern diet, rich gravel. boys often operated stone surgeons period mortality rate 30%. operation called lithotomy took three five minutes perform. anesthesia used, instead surgeons relied child fainting pain extraction stone. often, parents turned first family, friends, neighbors, medical advice, even local blacksmith fee would set bones humans well animals.as specialty pediatrics greek child healing) yet emerge, children treated small adults hospitals kept wards adult men women. charitable institutions opened early modern period, example, children's hospital norwich 1621, tended children abandoned parents orphaned, sick youngsters. largest institution orphans foundling hospital london, opened 1741. also medical discoveries helped children youth period, notably, inoculation vaccination smallpox. starting 1960s several scholars argued early modern parents tried invest much emotion money) child reached age survival likely. high birth rates, accompanied high death rates children age ten years old, meant family life fragile uncertain. yet parent-child relationship seems strong early modern period age, former ideas emotional indifference eighteenth century widely questioned scholars. population hard struggle existence title: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu on Small Pox in Turkey [Letter] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city lady mary wortley montagu small pox turkey [letter]annotation lady mary wortley montagu (1689-1762) wife british ambassador turkey. 1715 survived terribly scarred smallpox brother died disease. fascinated culture ottoman empire 1717 described turkish practice inoculating healthy children weakened strain smallpox confer immunity virulent strains disease. immediately seven-year old son inoculated turkey return england, daughter publicly inoculated royal court george popularize technique. partially successful inoculation continued dangerous often resulted death scarring infected children. [full text available online.]sourcemontagu, lady mary wortley. letters right honourable lady w—y written travels europe, asia africa persons distinction, men letters, &c. different parts europe. aix: anthony henricy, 1796, 167-69, letter 36, mrs. s.c. adrianople, 1717. reproduced online: fordham university, "lady mary wortley montagu (1689-1762): smallpox vaccination turkey," modern history sourcebook, http://www.fordham.edu/halsall/mod/montagu-smallpox.html (accessed march 26, 2008). annotated lynda payne.primary source texta propos distempers, going tell thing, make wish small-pox, fatal, general amongst us, entirely harmless, invention engrafting, term give set old women, make business perform operation, every autumn, month september, great heat abated. people send one another know family mind small-pox; make parties purpose, met (commonly fifteen sixteen together) old woman comes nut-shell full matter best sort small-pox, asks vein please opened. immediately rips open offer large needle gives pain common scratch) puts vein much matter lie upon head needle, binds little wound hollow bit shell, manner opens four five veins. grecians commonly superstition opening one middle forehead, one arm, one breast, mark sign cross; ill effect, wounds leaving little scars, done superstitious, chuse legs, part arm concealed. children young patients play together rest day, perfect health eighth. fever begins seize keep beds two days, seldom three. rarely twenty thirty faces, never mark, eight days time well illness. wounded, remains running sores distemper, don't doubt great relief every year, thousands undergo operation, french ambassador says pleasantly, take small-pox way diversion, take waters countries. example one died may believe well satisfied safety experiment, since intend try dear little son. patriot enough take pains bring useful invention fashion england, fail write doctors particularly knew one thought virtue enough destroy considerable branch revenue, good mankind. distemper beneficial expose resentment, hardy wight undertake put end perhaps live return, may, however, courage war upon occasion, admire heroism heart friend, etc. etc. title: Gin Lane (1751) [Engraving] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city gin lane (1751) [engraving] annotationthis one best-known prints famous artist, william hogarth. designed support british government's attempt regulate price popularity drinking gin (known geneva) gin act 1751. print accompanied following verse: gin, cursed fiend, fury fraught, makes human race prey. enters deadly draught steal life away. virtue truth, driv'n despair rage compells fly, cherishes hellish care theft, murder, perjury. damned cup! vitals preys liquid fire contains, madness heart conveys, rolls thro' veins. gin originally marketed medicine upset stomachs netherlands. imported britain 1689 quickly became choice drink poor. many distilled sold homes. historians claim 1750, one every fifteen houses london sold children would often sent buy gin parents sampled also regularly given calm babies. hogarth shows poverty, public drunkenness, crime, resulted cheap availability gin. shocking figure gin lane drunken mother. may partially based real person, judith dufour. due mother's neglect, two-year old child taken placed workhouse. dufour reclaimed child shortly afterwards strangled left body ditch. sold clothes workhouse provided child pennies, used money buy gin. dufour publicly hanged murder.sourcegin lane (1751). etching engraving william hogarth. print collection, title: London's Bill of Mortality (December 1664-December 1665) [Official Document] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city london's bill mortality (december 1664-december 1665) [official document] annotationduring great outbreak bubonic plague black death hot summer 1665 london, special bills mortality issued listed causes death. mid-july thousand deaths week reported handbills stuck public places warn people plague growing. rich fled city poor option died droves. shown front bill lists final count year 1665 memento mori remember die, written across top skeletons representing death around edges. second bill lists number deaths london one week september 1665. shows 7,165 people died plague. deaths recorded point high infant mortality early modern england; 17 chrisomes, infants died first month life; 121 teeth, infants died still teething. fifteen children died worms parasites body. several fevers also mentioned 42 women died childbed fever, bacterial infection giving birth, 101 people succumbed spotted fever (probably typhus).sourcewellcome library, "london's dreadful visitation: collection bills mortality present year: beginning 27th december 1664 ending 19th december following: also general whole years bill. according report made king's excellent majesty company parish-clerks london," wellcome library, wellcome collection, http://images.wellcome.ac.uk/ixbin/hixclient.exe?miropac=l0030700 http://images.wellcome.ac.uk/ixbin/hixclient.exe?miropac=l0030701 (accessed october 1, 2008). annotated lynda payne. cite source lynda payne, "health england (16th–18th c.)," children youth history, title: John Evelyn's Diary, 1658 [Literary Excerpt] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city john evelyn's diary, 1658 [literary excerpt]annotationthe english lawyer john evelyn (1620-1706) kept diary nearly 50 years recorded grief death four children. john mary evelyn eight children altogether: richard (1652–8), john standsfield (1653–4), john (1655–99), george (1657–8), richard (1664), mary (1665–85), elizabeth (1667–85) susanna (1669–1754). susanna outlived parents. january 1658 eldest son dick (richard) fell ill quartan ague fever, sweats fits, finally died. physicians sent london bitterly cold weather prevented arriving time help little dick. evelyn's devastation loss son shows well painfully records dick's age 5 years, 5 months, 3 days. angrily blames death servants keeping dick hot great fire blankets. evelyn educated man interested science natural philosophy called 17th century. may led somewhat unusual decision attend autopsy dick. findings liver growne large spleen, suggest possibly rickets malaria cause dick's death. following month evelyn recorded death yet another son youngest, george, aged seven weeks.sourcejohn evelyn, "1658," geocities, http://www.geocities.com/paris/leftbank/1914/ed_1658.html#1658 (accessed march 28, 2008). annotated lynda payne.primary source text1658 january 27 six fitts quartan ague pleased god visite deare child dick fitts extreame, especiale one sides, rigor hot fitt, fell greate intollerable sweate, surpriz'd aboundance vapours ascending head, fell fatal symptoms, help hand able recover spirits, long painefull conflict, falling sleep thought, coverd warme, (though midst severe frosty season) greate fire roome; plainely expired, unexpressable griefe affliction. sent physitians lond, whilst yet life river frozen coach brake way ere got mile house; artificial help failing, natural strength exhausted, lost prettiest, dearest child, ever parents 5 years <5 months> 3 days old years even tender age, aprodigie witt, understanding; beauty body angel, endowments mind, incredible rare hopes. 30 saturday following, sufferd physitians opened: dr. needham dr. welles, come three days little time ere expired, past help, opinion suffocated woman maide tended covered hott blankets lay cradle, neere excessive hot fire close roome; wife long come come lifting blanket, quite coved cradle, taking first notice wonderfull fresh colour, hardly hearing breath heave, soone perceived neere overcome heate sweate, doubtlesse child farr gon, could make heare, open eyes, though life apparently gave something make sneeze ineffectivly: open'd found membranous substance growing cavous part liver, somewhat neere edge compasse 3 inches, ought liver fixed onely three strong ligaments, far distant part; confidently affirm'd, child tis vulgarly cald) liver-growne, thence sicknesse frequent complaint side: indeede liver splen exceedingly large &c: caused body cofin'd lead reposited night, 8 clock church deptford, accompanied divers relations neighbours, among distributed rings ___ dominus abstulit: intending (god willing) transported owne body, interrd dormitorie wotton churh deare native county surry, lay bones mingle dust fathers 1 god gracious make fit blessed child ends joy life, go even mourning grave: l. jesus sanctifie others afflictions: amen: february 15 afflicting hand god still upon us, pleased also take away us morning youngest sonn george 7 weeks languishing nurse, breeding teeth, ending dropsie: gods holy buried deptford church 17th following:____ march ben severest winter, man alive knowne england: crowes feet frozen prey: ilands ice inclosed fish foule frozen, persons boates: 1 richard's body never moved. epitaph still st. nicholas, deptford. e gave similar account son's life preface translation golden book john chrysostom, see p. 119 459. 2 foeffees 'trustees holding land charitable uses' (de beer). 3'with great pomp', acts apostles, xxv.23. e's translation, see p. 459. cite source lynda payne, "health england (16th–18th c.)," children youth history, item #166, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/166 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1450-1770, title: Rubeola Vulgaris (measles) [Still Image] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city rubeola vulgaris (measles) [still image] annotationrobert willan (1757-1812) physician practiced london. like sydenham fascinated relation weather epidemics kept strict records occurred several years. particularly interested diseases children carefully observed rashes pustules developed stages skin. first volume book, cutaneous diseases, published 1798-1808 widely admired medical world. historians generally agree book launched modern specialty dermatology. volume notable beautiful graphic colored plates. willan closely supervised creation 1812 preparing second volume publication, willan sadly died tuberculosis. plate 20 shows young child's face arm covered rash characteristic measles. often severe disfiguring disease early modern england could result death infection spread tissue bone, resulting gangrene.sourcewillan, robert. cutaneous diseases. n.p., 1808. wellcome collection, "pate 20: cutaneous diseases," wellcome library, http://library.wellcome.ac.uk/doc_wtd015575.html (accessed october 13, 2008). cite source lynda payne, "health england (16th–18th c.)," children youth occurred several years. particularly interested diseases children carefully observed rashes pustules developed stages skin. first volume book, cutaneous diseases, published 1798-1808 widely admired title: Infanticide Trial Transcript from the Old Bailey of Elizabeth Taylor of Clerkenwell, London, June 1734 [Trial Record] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city infanticide trial transcript old bailey elizabeth taylor clerkenwell, london, june 1734 [trial record]annotationinfanticide killing baby punishable hanging early modern england. unlike married women accused infanticide, mere fact single women tried conceal death babies considered proof murder infanticide act 1624. single woman's recourse try prove baby born dead killed difficult many women gave birth witnesses. mid-18th century research historians, know 70% women indicted infanticide servants occupation age 16. also know trend century progressed juries find women innocent guilty crime infanticide unless clear proof murdered baby. perhaps beginning see young women victims rather criminals. case elizabeth taylor shows fear shame single women felt became pregnant lack privacy lives servants. elizabeth speak trial, instead witnesses prosecution called first judge jury ask questions whether elizabeth hid pregnancy baby. dinah beaven testifies baby appear murdered. probably midwife acts expert witness. finally, prisoner newgate elizabeth states found baby items sewn elizabeth's coat prisoners took (new prisoners often fleeced inmates used money goods bribe gaolers providing basics bad bread, foul water, old straw bedding.) fact elizabeth made provision baby strongest proof intend kill court let go. [full text available online.]sourcemay, allyson n. first denied infanticide trials old bailey." women history: voices early modern england, edited valerie frith, 31–2. concord, ontario: irwin could bear sight creature. margaret thomas goldsmith, deposed effect. elizabeth windsor. prisoner never told child, ill, great deal water come much better. child first found, denied owned afterwards. title: The Graham Children (1742) [Painting] - Children and Youth in History | Health in England (16th–18th c.)
content: teaching module health england (16th–18th c.) lynda payne, university missouri-kansas city graham children (1742) [painting] annotationthis beautiful life-size painting four children william hogarth, also specialized engravings gin lane. commissioned daniel graham, rich apothecary (pharmacist) royal family chelsea hospital london. shows daniel's three children second wife, mary crisp, mary's daughter, henrietta, first marriage. children left right) infant thomas sitting gold-leaf gilded go-cart (age 2); henrietta blue dress holding two cherries (age 9); anna maria flower-print dress (age 5); richard plays sérinette bird organ (age 7). hogarth patron foundling hospital london painted portraits many children skill led commissions one. first glance hogarth literally paints childhood time innocence happiness. clues point fragility children's lives. clock mantelpiece decorated figure cupid holding scythe, symbol death. standing beside hourglass, yet another symbol mortality. cat gazes greedily caged bird remind us predators even happy scene fact thomas died hogarth completing portrait posthumous representation infant, still alive age two. crossed carnations thomas's feet symbolize like flowers, life faded quickly, dove go-cart may represent soul flying heaven.sourcehogarth, william. graham children. oil canvas, 1742. annotated lynda payne. cite source (1749-1823) physician rural gloucestershire. like lady mary wortley montagu learnt widely known folk remedy protect smallpox. smallpox cases increasing 18th century mortality rate 40%. least 30% using cowpox protect smallpox, vaccination vacca, latin word cow. fact historians evidence local farmers carried procedure jenner first medical man publish findings cowpox preventive remedy smallpox title: Decameron [Literary Excerpt] - Children and Youth in History | Children during the Black Death
content: teaching module children black death shona kelly wray, university missouri-kansas city decameron [literary excerpt]annotationgiovanni boccaccio provided famous description happened black death italy. report behavior florentines plague entered city spring 1348 serves introduction frame collection 100 tales entitled decameron. epidemic provides pretext group young men women leave florence retire pleasant villa countryside entertain telling stories 10 days. though novella range bawdy pious, story telling light hearted keeping purpose restorative diversion. boccaccio's introduction, however, different tone. author relates precise detail gruesome symptoms disease horrific circumstances took place florence epidemic swept town disrupting forms normal human relations. government, medical care, neighborliness broke people secluded restricted diet, others recklessly gave pleasure, still others tried behave temperately. distressing according boccaccio, collapse families abandonment children. english translation followed original italian.sourceboccaccio, giovanni. decameron. translated g. h. mcwilliam. 2nd ed. london: penguin books, 1995, 8–9. annotated shona kelly wray.primary source textit merely question one citizen avoiding another, people almost invariably neglecting neighbours rarely never visiting relatives, addressing distance; scourge implanted great terror hearts men women brothers abandoned brothers, uncles nephews, sisters brothers, many cases wives deserted husbands. even worse, almost incredible fact fathers mothers refused nurse assist children, though belong [original italian] e lasciamo stare che l'uno cittadino l'altro schifasse e quasi niuno vicino avesse dell'altro cura e parenti insieme rade volte o non mai si visitassero e di lontano: era con sí fatto spavento questa tribulazione entrata ne' petti degli uomini e delle donne, che l'un fratello l'altro abbandonava e il zio il nepote e la sorella il fratello e spesse volte la donna il suo marito; e, che maggior cosa e quasi non credibile, li padri e lemadri figliuoli, quasi loro non fossero, di visitare e di servire schifavano. cite source shona kelly wray, "children black death," children youth history, item #167, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/167 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1000-1500 ce, black death, bologna, europe, italy, legal documents, literary sources, title: Italian accounts of the Black Death [Personal Accounts] - Children and Youth in History | Children during the Black Death
content: teaching module children black death shona kelly wray, university missouri-kansas city italian accounts black death [personal accounts]annotationseveral chroniclers wrote black death town region. described symptoms disease, generally called mortality," arrived portents warning east, many people killed. accounts long embellished descriptions townspeople's actions, brief, providing little dates plague's entry mortality rates, usually inflated. description families children, present, often quite short. chroniclers wrote either latin italian. note terms use original language well modern english translation.source[1] chronicle marco battagli rimini, "marcha di marco battagli da rimini." rerum italicarum scriptores [ris]. vol. 16, part 3. edited f. massèra, 54. città di castello, 1912, 54. [2] chronicle guglielmo cortusi padua, "chronica de novitatibus padue et lombardie guilielmi de cortusis." ris. vol. 12, part 5. edited b. pagnin, 120–21. [3] città di castello, 1941, 120–121. tuscan chronicle account, "storie pistoresi." ris. vol. 11, part 5. edited s. barbi, 235. città di castello, 1906, 235. [4] chronicle agnolo di tura del grasso, "cronica senese di agnolo di tura del grasso." ris. vol. 15, part 6. edited ed. lisini f. iacometti, 555. bologna, 1935, 555. annotated shona kelly wray.primary source text1. chronicle marco battagli riminithe chronicler rimini, northeastern coastal region, wrote "father fled son became sick, brother avoided brother, wife husband, thus healthy fled ill." [original latin] pater postea infirmum filium evitabat, frater fratrem, uxor virum, et sic de singulis sani infirmos penitus evitabant. 2. chronicle guglielmo cortusi padua guglielmo cortusi wrote city chronicle padua plague "wife fled embrace dear husband, father son, brother brother." [original latin]uxor fugiebat amplexum cari viri, pater filii, frater fratris. 3. tuscan chronicle account storie pistoresi announce tuscany especially pisa, "father abandoned son, children abandoned mother father, one brother abandoned [original italian] lo padre abbandonava li figliuoli, e' figliuoli lo padre e la madre, e l'uno fratello l'altro. 4. chronicle agnolo di tura del grasso sienese chronicler, agnolo di tura del grasso, left poignant account plague. possible," says describe horrible thing human speech." noting truncated burial practices, without priest, appropriate liturgy bells, discusses subject child abandonment familiar words "father abandoned child, wife husband, one brother abandon children tells us buried five children hands. title: Health Ordinances of Pistoia, 1348 [Legal Document] - Children and Youth in History | Children during the Black Death
content: teaching module children black death shona kelly wray, university missouri-kansas city health ordinances pistoia, 1348 [legal document]annotationcities italy passed legislation aimed preventing reducing effects plague. since scientific view plague caused miasma bad air, measures targeted rotting smelling matter, viz. cloth could retain miasma spread disease passed person person, dead bodies, rotting meat. thus, ordinances regulated burials restricted mobility cloth activities butchers tanners. ordinances really new black death; governments simply re-enforced sanitary legislation effect cities 13th century.sourcechiappelli, alberto, ed. "gli ordinamenti sanitari del comune di pistoia contro la pestilenzia del 1348." archivio storico italiano. ser. 4, 20 (1887): 8–22. quoted horrox, rosemary, ed. trans. black death. manchester: manchester university press, 1994, 195–96. annotated shona kelly wray.primary source textselections health ordinances pistoia [2 may, 1348] one, whether pistoia elsewhere, shall dare presume bring fetch pistoia, whether person agent, old linen woolen clothes, male female clothing bedspreads; penalty 200 pence, cloth burnt public piazza pistoia official discovered however shall lawful citizens pistoia travelling within pistoia territories take linen woolen cloths use wear, provided pack fardle weighing 30 lb. less. bodies dead shall removed place death enclosed wooden box, lid planks nailed stench escape, covered one pall, coverlet cloth; penalty 50 pence paid heirs deceased heirs, nearest kinsmen male line. avoid foil stench comes dead bodies grave shall dug two half arms length deep, reckoned pistoia; penalty 10 pence anyone digging ordering digging grave infringes statute. living made ill rotten corrupt food, butcher retailer meat shall dare presume hang meat, keep sell meat storehouse counter [i.e. whole carcasses cannot displayed, joints meat]; penalty 10d. cite source shona kelly wray, "children black death," children youth history, item #167, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/167 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1000-1500 ce, black death, bologna, europe, italy, legal documents, literary sources, official documents introduction primary sources decameron [literary excerpt] italian accounts black death [personal accounts] health ordinances pistoia, 1348 [legal document] testament father black death testament mother black death testament elite wife black death testament elite husband black death will-making among general populace bologna 1348 [graph] dance dead [mural] dance death [woodcut] teaching strategies lesson plan document based question resources bibliography credits teaching module children black death shona kelly wray, university missouri-kansas city health ordinances pistoia, 1348 [legal document]annotationcities italy passed legislation aimed preventing reducing effects plague. since scientific view plague caused miasma bad air, measures targeted rotting smelling matter, viz. cloth could retain miasma spread disease passed person person, dead bodies, rotting meat. thus, ordinances regulated burials restricted mobility cloth activities butchers tanners. ordinances really new black death; title: Testament of a Father during the Black Death [Will] - Children and Youth in History | Children during the Black Death
content: teaching module children black death shona kelly wray, university missouri-kansas city testament father black death [will]annotationbelow offers window family life carinus, parchment worker, cartolarius, fairly common trade university town bologna. although many wealthy people left long complicated wills filled lists pious bequests, simple typical artisans despite modern assumptions contrary, commonly made wills medieval italy. according intestate law medieval italian towns, sons inherit patrimony equally daughters receive dowry, husbands could use married returned end marriage. carinus made ill beginning july wife, ursollina, newly widowed, made two weeks later. remain family married son, johannes, made july 30, naming unborn child heir wife pregnant time). petrobellus, brother mengorius, husband carinus ursollina's daughter, made day ursollina. notary parish priest present four wills. english translation left legalistic formulaic language.sourcearchivio di stato di bologna. memoriali. vol. 230, folio 98r. annotated shona kelly wray.primary source textin name christ, amen. july 8, 1348. figliocharius, known carinus, son late brother donisdei, parchment maker living parish san biagio bologna, healthy mind intellect, sick body, declares thus. leaves five lire benefit soul distributed heirs would seem best useful soul. five lire, five solidi [20 solidi equaled one lira] go gerardus, chaplain parish church san biagio, masses soul. leaves 20 solidi two nieces mina cicillia. returns wife dowry 130 lire modest dowry, typical artisan class] gives 20 lire well bed linen woolen clothes life time death. grants wife usufruct right use goods long lives home children, remains widow, ask dowry aforesaid legacies [i.e., 20 lire, bed clothing]. leaves daughter, isabeta, wife mengorius, son late brother ugollini parish san biagio, arranged dowry marriage mengorius. blessing [nomine benedictionis], also leaves 20 solidi. goods property, moveable immovable, rights actions, future, institutes universal heirs, three sons, thomas, johannes, bernardus. declares last cancels invalidates previous testament codicil. enacted testator's house parish san biagio, presence following witnesses: gerardus, parish priest san biagio, johannes son late albertinus, draper san biagio, petrus son late johannes, parchment maker san biagio, paulus son late ugollinus, parchment maker san biagio, bertone son late jacobus, armorer san biagio, guidone son late simone, parchment maker san biagio, tomas son late brother albertus, parchment maker san biagio, bonacosa son late leyus, draper san biagio, benvenutus son late gerardinus, baker san biagio. written notary, johannes laurentii stephanii. notary johannes albertini, designated proctor sick testator, went office memoriali day register testament. cite source shona kelly wray, "children black death," children youth history, item #167, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/167 (accessed august 10, 2021). beginning july wife, ursollina, newly widowed, made two weeks later. remain family married son, johannes, made july 30, naming unborn child heir wife pregnant time). petrobellus, brother mengorius, (note grand-daughters get money grandmother grandsons, presumably heirs father.) following wife's death, butcher sick plague make named guardian daughter chadiana take charge young grandchildren benefit estate heir. aided executor two clerics guardian notary, whose young daughters phylippinus provided dowry. noted family servant lived served epidemic. witnesses–their neighbors–were present testaments butcher wife. note also testators left cloth bequests epidemic. apparently concerned clothes retaining miasma anti-plague ordinances government.sourcearchivio di stato di bologna, title: Introduction - Children and Youth in History | Ancient China
content: teaching module ancient china anne kinney, university virginia introductionthe unprecedented interest child assumed unique importance han period set motion convergence historically-specific conditions: (1) establishment qin dynasty (221-207 bce) development han times (206 bce-220 ce) merit-based civil service, increased educational occupational opportunities boys moving social ladder; (2) frequency children came throne unprepared govern; (3) attempt confucian project advising women various methods child-rearing instruction young children; (4) effort educate girls proper roles society increasing anxiety influence women political events; (5) growing influence han confucianism, stressed early education argued establishment large-scale public school system; (6) new centrality correlative occult thought linked child cosmic processes extension major disciplines intellectual inquiry; (7) formation textual canon, defined cultural mastery established clear standard measure achievement; (8) emergence biographical writing took account significant events childhood. follows elaboration factors forces led emergence child topic significant cultural attention. contributed rising recognition children's important han society consolidation unified empire depended part meritocratic system advancement civil military officials. large-scale complex activities agenda centralized state required ruler take stock utilize, develop potential human resources fullest. thus, qin times, see new imperial government registering, taxing, demanding labor military service household empire according age gender family members. also see enactment laws punishing infanticide, infant abandonment, filicide period order husband state resources. energetic visionary first emperor qin died 209 bce son proved unequal task imperial rule, qin fell four years later. first han emperor, liu bang, emerged victorious wars followed qin's collapse, han faced tasks training installing bureaucracy, additional responsibility inquiring reasons behind qin's downfall rectifying errors. end, increasing number intellectuals urged han rulers take care education heirs throne, ensure boys replaced would govern wisely maintain health continuity dynasty. advised women various methods child-rearing instruction young children, sooner better, saw early childhood training crucial development accomplished adult. also called establishment public school system prepare boys civil service. recent precipitous demise qin fragility han empire opening years dynasty provided vivid reminder stake gradually worked train imperial intellectual attention childhood means stabilizing fortifying imperial rule. contrast diverse plentiful materials boys han sources, see little intellectual engagement issues specifically concerned girls. han times, women played fairly small role civil service military. consequently education girls attract nearly level attention devoted intellectual development boys. poignant example story empress deng small child, mother scolded attending closely classical learning needlework asked thought preparing post imperial academy erudite. nevertheless, 74 bce onward, increased anxiety influence women political events threat posed dynastic stability resulted efforts educate girls proper roles society, using models emulation lives exemplary women antiquity. impossible say many girls young women outside court literati circles received sort training, know goal female education differed boys. girls instructed ambitions sake moral, intellectual professional development fathers, brothers, husbands, sons. still, toward end former han, ambitious men gentry increasingly appraised confucian morals, womenfolk also came closer scrutiny. time, drawing imperial qin han confucian models, government made sporadic efforts reach subjects attempt bind closely ruling house. endeavor included women girls court gentry, broader base female subjects, recognized way special grants awards values chastity obedience. confucian education, reverence institutions privileged elders, ancestors, worthies antiquity, promoted study classical texts moral exemplars ages past gradually shape child according canonical molds. thinkers associated daoist thought, hand, traced infant's life back cosmogonic process, linking workings laws nature rather power ancestors human artifice. theme precocious child, linked struggles worthy often obscure figures establishment divinely sanctioned order, another reflection sensibilities qin han empires. replaced, varying degrees, aristocratic entitlement system ranks based merit. merit system sought adult males also young boys, groomed increasingly earlier ages bureaucratic positions recommended response occasional imperial edicts sought youths extraordinary gifts great promise. biographical writings han reveal similar new fascination intellectual capacities young children. conclusion, dynamic convergence variety historical conditions led children became important topics intellectual engagement significant subjects han times. single creation unified empire momentous profound impetus unprecedented focus children early china. key themes module (1) nature vs nurture (e.g., wisdom virtue inborn vs life-long cultivation learning virtue); (2) separating sexes gender differentiation: "inner" (private/domestic) realm women vs "outer" (public/official) realm men; (3) low status child gerontocratic culture vs child embodiment cosmic process heavenly order; (4) child's low status family vs child valued property state. cite source anne kinney, "ancient china," children youth history, item #187, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/187 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1000 bce-300 title: Isn't she a little young? Sex with a minor. Don't go there. [Billboard] - Children and Youth in History | Age of Consent Laws
content: teaching module age consent laws stephen robertson, university sydney, australia isn't little young? sex minor. don't go [billboard] annotationthis billboard erected across american state virginia summer 2004 part state health department campaign aimed reducing statutory rape crime sex underage girl). napkins, stickers, coasters, matchbooks bearing message distributed bars restaurants young men congregated. followed similar public education campaigns mounted states late 1990s. ads billboards connecticut 1994 depicted men prison accompanied slogan, "rob cradle get brand new crib." ads california 1997 featured young man saying, "nobody told sex could law. statutory rape? never heard voiceover added, "sex minor major crime you're adult sex minor—someone 18—you'll major time." ad ended sound jail door slamming shut. virginia department health motivated concern teenage pregnancy, perception enforcing age consent would reduce number girls became pregnant older men. connection made age consent public health problem. billboard highlights belief education awareness law could shape public opinion behavior.source"isn't little young?" virginia department health: sexual violence prevention, http://www.vahealth.org/civp/sexualviolence/statutoryrape.asp (accessed november 28, 2007). cite source stephen robertson, "age consent laws," children youth history, item #230, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/230 (accessed august 10, 2021). tags 1000-1500 ce, 1450-1770, 1750-1914, 1900-1945, advertising, age consent, boy, boys, europe, girl, government documents, law, legal documents introduction primary sources trial stephen arrowsmith (1678) [trial record] violation virgins" [newspaper article] petition raise age consent (1887) [petition] "review age-of-consent legislation texas"[magazine article, 1895] speech defending increased age consent india (1891) [speech transcript] adolescent sexual experimentation crime [commentary] jailbait (1957) [song lyrics] u.s. supreme court decision justifying gender-based age consent laws [legal document] rejection higher age consent homosexual acts [legal decision] isn't little young? title: Petition to Raise the Age of Consent (1887) [Petition] - Children and Youth in History | Age of Consent Laws
content: teaching module age consent laws stephen robertson, university sydney, australia petition raise age consent (1887) [petition]annotationpetitions played major role campaigns raise age consent represented way women, vote, seek legislative action. petition, drafted leaders women's christian temperance union (wctu) social purity reformers new york city, circulated organization's state local branches u.s. thousands forms submitted state federal legislatures; one petition raise age consent district columbia alone contained 200,000 signatures. wctu also solicited letters support legislators prominent citizens, organized mass meetings, lectures, speeches, sought favorable press coverage. petition deliberately use term "age consent" instead wctu used "age protection," members found "less objectionable." petitions notable several reasons: addition sexual violence, highlights new scenarios justifications increased age consent; also draws link age consent age majority, 20 years age period, girl could enter contracts control property.sourcewomen's christian temperance union. fluctuating attendance rates school aged children select number schools buenos aires 1815 1831. complete list port city’s schools; represents schools maintained complete attendance records. 1815, buenos aires 13 elementary schools 1,200 students, accounted approximately five percent city’s school aged children. clearly, children attending public educational institutions large numbers. end late 19th century, numbers improved, marginally. 1870s, instance, 107 public schools buenos aires (along 120 private schools), taught 7,700 students. meant 22 percent city’s school aged children attended title: Benjamín Montes with Bourgan, Funge, and Company [Labor Contract] - Children and Youth in History | Love & Authority in Argentina (19th c)
content: teaching module love authority argentina (19th c) jesse hingson, jacksonville university benjamín montes bourgan, funge, company [labor contract]annotation since 1810, social critics buenos aires long concerned young people lower classes—especially young men—exercising greater independence within home. decline parental authority, alarmed sight growing numbers young people potential source disorder, looked state solutions. result, police granted broad authority place youngsters jobs would keep streets supply businesses homes badly needed laborers. 1841, benjamín montes, minor, signed contract bourgan, funge, company, hat factory buenos aires, work three years apprentice. first glance, terms contract seem harsh. limited montes's mobility, governed behavior factory, work probably grueling. however, type arrangement also illustrative weakening parental authority favor state authority. montes's mother, juana maría olivera, clearly transferred power son company (mothers could sign contracts male heads households unavailable). kinds jobs provided greater opportunities young people, like benjamín, gain enough income make life reached adulthood.sourcearchivo general de la nación, x-31-9-5, policía, 1823–50. reprinted szuchman, mark d. order, family, community buenos aires, 1810-1860. stanford, ca: stanford university press, 1988. annotated jesse hingson.primary source textlong live federation! general department police contract buenos aires, march 1841 year 32 liberty, 25 independence, 12 argentine confederation undersigned, accordance law november 17, 1821, agree upon following articles: art. 1. principals bourgan, funge, co., declare accordance proposal made us doña juana maría olivera accept son, benjamín montes, apprentice factory, receiving promises good conduct, agreed term three years, beginning time commence training making plush silk hats level perfections currently produced. art. 2. commit maintenance give shelter thirty-five pesos monthly agreed-upon term three years. art. 3. benjamín montes commits perform duties customary factory sundays holidays, required maintain cleanliness, punctual working hours, obligations away factory days work, comply religious obligations. art. 4. benjamín montes, apprentice, obligated maintain good order harmony members house agreed-upon term three years, obey without hesitation whatever foreman may ask things related factory good order), event benjamín flee factory, obligation mother find bring back, two days added contracted period service every day remains fugitive. art. 5. benjamín montes, fully cognizant everything contained previous four articles, approval lady mother, declare day forth enter apprenticeship agreed upon term three years hat factory messrs. bourgan bunge (sic) co. conditions obligations herein expressed, proof [agreement] add signatures one lady mother…and effect hereby authorized chief police. cite source "love authority argentina (19th c)," children youth "shocking disaster cambridge: three children burn death." newspaper article. 1884. mrs. osborne, shopping town, put infant child locked bedroom, would disturbed two children [aged 2 4 years]. seeing everything safe, fire house since breakfast time, shut boy girl kitchen, proceeded town business. usual thing mrs. osborne shut children, believing would find way river, chains distant. coroner referred boys’ habit using matches described mother, doubt fire originated boy getting hold matches occasion, way setting fire clothes paper may lying though children might left neighbor. juryman informed coroner neighbors vicinity, unfortunate people position employ girl look house absence. verdict accidental death burning returned. parliamentary debate "juvenile depravity suppression bill." 1896. "mr. w. hutchinson: number young children amongst us painfully demoralised young, policeman could think interfering children suffering so-called liberty run unto utter license lawlessness; arising largely parental carelessness positive neglect. mere children got together street-corner dark verandah; talked, listened talk, sweet babble childhood, mixed laugh innocence, talk need described; got temptations kinds understood disastrous results certainly followed. ventured suggest young children dealt come advanced age. bill took note incipiency vice, yet mischief began. bill police bill, pure simple; needed out-worn still perfectly true axiom prevention better cure." w. e. spencer, inspector schools, taranaki education office, 1898. causes bad attendance, exclusive bad roads inclement weather, may classed two heads (1) home circumstances pupils, (2) school authorities. first head may mention parental indifference neglect excessive work required children tender years. know milking season children milk many ten cows every morning, come school arrive late, fatigued unfit work day. second heads ample scope attraction. school building ill-lighted, gloomy, depressing one cannot wonder children preferring stay away preferring sunshine dulness [sic]. means let schools cheerful, bright, attractive, let walls covered interesting instructive charts pictures arouse sustain curiosity. let first impressions school-day pleasant ones. let us means children may amuse recesses school opens, possible, come early regularly brief interval companionship amusement. personality teacher well-known factor producing good bad attendance. lack sympathy, harshness, carelessness, incompetency inevitably lower attendance. female interviewee (born 1897). interview colonial childhoods oral history project. brother born 12 night born, mother "would like go round stay mrs andrews?" stayed night came home morning. mrs andrews "oh, go home went home. wasn't far living petone. got side saw beautiful baby basket, chair, dining room saw somebody rushing round starched apron cap head thought, "well, "who's basket?" "that's little brother." "oh," "well i'll go tell mother." "don't dare open door. mother ill." well, nearly 12 idea wild world brother come got anything else think quite wrong. told must naïve idiot something, don't know never noticed mother different baby." code honour new zealand boys' diary. 1936. qualities real man woman one overgrown baby? faithful duties god? pure thought, word action? study imitate greatest men women world? strength eat, drink play moderation forms make better morally, intellectually physically? determined work betterment fellow men? new zealander, proud privilege, yet humble enjoyment scorn dishonesty, whatsoever form degree, petty mean altogether unworthy family high traditions school empire. things temperate eating, play, rest, work, exercising always one true discipline discipline self. regard coarseness thought, language, action, belittling degrading, always altogether beneath dignity future citizen fair dominion. cheerfully yield reasonable prompt obedience elders, particularly parents; show like respect rules school, by-laws town, laws country, since know rules laws needlessly made. punctual orderly cheerful. keep promises. grudge effort, matter small great task, remembering best good enough. ever pure true, daily trust remember hands children to-day world to-morrow strive unworthy sacred trust. new zealand school photographs 1950 1964. sanitarium weet-bix packet [advertisement], 1990s. cite title: Introduction - Children and Youth in History | New Zealand Childhoods (18th–20th c.)
content: teaching module new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.) jeanine graham, university waikato new zealand (retired) introductionthe impact colonization childhood experiences new zealand diverse enduring. although never formal apartheid system, biculturalism continues commonly characteristic maori indigenous ancestry) pakeha (non-maori, generally european descent). recently pakeha children grew little knowledge familiarity maori language custom. conversely, children identified maori little option engage language, practices, values colonial regime. many urban-raised lost access cultural heritage process. late 20th century, combined influences maori cultural renaissance debates nature post-colonial new zealand identity, anglo-saxon assumptions inherent cultural superiority challenged. culture circumstance, location, time period, family support structures shaped nature antipodean childhoods. formal colonization began 1840, great britain declared sovereignty islands inhabitants. involvement 500 tribal leaders discussions signing treaty waitangi year reflected several decades encounters. preceding half-century, coastal tribes interacted seamen, sealers, whalers, traders, missionaries introduced language, food, material culture, values, diseases. european men sexual relations indigenous women also contributed gene pool. children liaisons normally brought within mother's community. apart occasional trader shore-based whaler lived long-term local woman, european fathers generally unknown progeny. offspring necessarily disadvantaged. tribal identification, traced whakapapa (family trees), incorporated ancestry parents, providing indigenous children extensive network relations, allegiances, obligations. immigrant children grew comparable family support. created two three generations living traditional lifestyles evolved seven centuries descendants eastern polynesian voyagers adapted new zealand's temperate climate. changes caused european values, policies, diseases abrupt. end 1850s, settler indigenous populations roughly equal, 59,000 56,000 respectively. rapid influx europeans next two decades, largely response gold discoveries, public works schemes, assisted immigration policies changed demographic balance. time, rapid land alienation islands conflict north land sales sovereignty issues destroyed economic independence potential prosperity many tribes. children cultures affected upheavals era. measure charitable government aid normally available "deserving"—victorian distinctions worthy unworthy recipients assistance well-entrenched colonial thinking. maori communities adversely affected confiscation sale productive land much less likely receive assistance event crop failure, poverty, disease. even native school system, established 1867, required village communities contribute land portion costs institution. state-funded secular elementary schools, open children 7 13 years age, compulsory attendance enforced maori pakeha turn century, concentrated numeracy, literacy, physical fitness. schools also served powerful agencies socialization contemporary values citizenship, imperialism, loyalty british crown imparted. childhoods new zealand long reflected consequences external well internal events. lacking immunity introduced infections, indigenous population declined steadily, reaching lowest number (42,000 total new zealand population 743,000) mid-1890s. eventual demise widely predicted. yet gradual recovery occurred, despite disproportionately high maori death rate 1918 influenza epidemic. children cultures lost relatives friends carnage world war lived returned physically emotionally impaired. many youngsters also experienced economic hardship years great depression. state welfare, social security, education policies late 1930s following world war ii sought establish equal access services children, although government agencies initially slow recognize, respond major population shift occurring, young adults maori families moved en masse rural urban areas search better employment, lifestyles, living conditions. demand unskilled labor also encouraged many polynesian people leave pacific island homes work opportunities new zealand. schools main cities, auckland wellington especially, soon reflected greater cultural diversity brought urban communities maori pasifika families (tongan, samoan, nuiean cook islanders, instance), trend would accelerate latter decades 20th century asian migrants became significant minority group total population. insidious inequalities colonialism yet fully redressed, inclusive educational curriculum provides new zealand's children much richer understanding influence available earlier generations colonial-born. cite source "new title: Lesson Plan: Constructing an Author’s Attitude from Tone Words - Children and Youth in History | New Zealand Childhoods (18th–20th c.)
content: teaching module new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.) jeanine graham, university waikato new zealand (retired) lesson plan: constructing author’s attitude tone words ryba l. epstein time estimated: one 50-minute class grade level: 10th 12th grades objectives students learn identify tone words connotations; detect speaker's/author's attitude using tone connotation. materials projection online images, possible available, make transparencies two photos listed hook activity) copies primary source documents student class: adventure new zealand 1839 1844 (document 72) annual report native affairs, 1874 (document 91) paper, pencils/pens strategies hook project image apache children arrived carlisle indian industrial school image become acculturated. ask students quickly brainstorm descriptive words first picture second picture. ask students categorize impressions, looking specific words used describe positive negative attributes, attempt determine factors might underlie perceptions. discuss meaning "tone," "connotation," "attitude." group activity next, divide class small groups (three five students) pass copies two primary sources new zealand. group choose recorder write group's responses. students go documents, underlining words reflect tone connotation. next, students make two lists words underlined: one positive one negative tone words. students analyze lists determine factors generally determined authors sources considered positive negative. (usually, students decide behavior indicated increased acceptance western culture life-styles seen positive authors.) finally, group write one two sentences explaining attitude author, supporting opinion reference lists tone words. students also try identify underlying assumptions led author attitude. example: wakefield implies maoris married children westerners superior not—as supported use words superior," "strikingly comely," "remarkable cleanliness order." word choice indicates attitude assumes superiority western standards beauty cleanliness indigenous people. recorders group write group's final statements board. class discuss similarities differences statements, checking validity appropriate support statement's opinion tone words cited. homework: students write paragraph trying identify underlying reasons attitudes expressed authors relating attitudes broader 19th-century european social cultural beliefs. differentiation advanced students able students, direct website carlisle indian industrial school http://home.epix.net/~landis/histry.html. list examples tone words primary source documents embedded site. similar words used primary sources new zealand? similarities inferred two educational systems' attitudes toward indigenous children future roles "modern" society? ask students write letter mr. locke (document 91) describing carlisle school's successes new zealand minister native affairs. cite source "new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.)," children youth history, item #93, https://cyh.rrchnm.org/items/show/93 (accessed august 10, 2021). annotationthe colonial childhoods oral history project (ccohp) comprises recorded interviews 165 new zealanders, male female, maori pakeha, majority born 1903. interviews focus period individual’s title: Adventure in New Zealand, from 1839 to 1844 [Book Excerpt] - Children and Youth in History | New Zealand Childhoods (18th–20th c.)
content: teaching module new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.) jeanine graham, university waikato new zealand (retired) adventure new zealand, 1839 1844 [book excerpt]annotatione. j. wakefield 19 years age sailed england, 1839, new zealand company vessel, tory, secretary uncle, colonel william wakefield. wakefield oversee foundation company settlement cook strait region, country's capital city, wellington, located. land purchases tribal owners priority colonizing agency. exploration possible sites sides strait, wakefields made contact european whalers whose shore-based communities reflected years personal liaison local maori. adventure new zealand based upon detailed journals younger wakefield kept meticulously first visit country. source selection focuses ways children european fathers maori mothers perceived living civilized lifestyle consequence european influences. wakefield's comments overtly racist judgmental. simply reflects contemporary british viewpoint known "native" people, maori capable great "improvement"—providing, course, exposure beneficial attributes british society. domesticity te awaiti stark contrast widely-reported problems stemming alcohol licentiousness bay islands, long favorite harbor haven deep-sea whaling crews.sourcewakefield, edward jerningham. adventure new zealand, 1839 1844, account beginning british colonization islands.vol. 1. london: john murray, 1845, 44–45, 50. annotated jeanine graham.primary source textseptember 1, sunday [1839]. prayers board tory], landed visited whaling-town te-awa-iti [te awaiti, queen charlotte's sound]. twenty houses presented view; walls generally constructed wattled supple-jack, called kareau, filled clay; roof thatched reeds; large unsightly chimney one ends, constructed either materials walls, stones heaped together rude masonry. dicky barrett's house, ware [whare] called maori native language, superior edifice, built sawn timber, floored lined inside, sheltered front ample veranda. long room half full natives whalers. wife e rangi, fine stately woman, gave us dignified welcome; pretty half-caste children laughed commented appearance, mother's relations, language. three girls adopted son old trader friend named jacky love, death-bed, regretted natives one married young chieftainess great rank, son dan treated universal respect kindness entitled character father rank mother. twenty-five half-caste children te-awa-iti. strikingly comely, many quite fair, light hair rosy cheeks; active hardy goats settlement also swarmed. women whalers remarkable cleanliness order preserved companion's house. dressed loose gowns printed calico, hair, generally fine, always clean well-combed. cite source "new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.)," title: 1996 New Zealand Census Information [Statistical Tables] - Children and Youth in History | New Zealand Childhoods (18th–20th c.)
content: teaching module new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.) jeanine graham, university waikato new zealand (retired) 1996 new zealand census information [statistical tables] annotationthese tables give details three health-related facets young new zealanders' lives interpreted data recorded 1996 census: levels educational qualification school leavers, unemployment rates, youth mortality. selection reflects particular area public concern, namely high number deaths, among young males especially, suicide, self-inflicted injury, motor vehicle accidents. 1996, suicides young people aged 15-24 years, represented 26.6% total suicide deaths, yet age group 15.6% total population. although number rate youth suicide declined 1995 2000, maori youth suicide rate continued approximately 50% higher non-maori rate. (see: youth suicide prevention information new zealand.) definition young new zealanders reflects social political changes recent decades, noticeably expectation longer period financial dependency. calculations tertiary student allowances, example, initially took parental income account student turned 25. yet legal drinking age reduced 20 18 1999, youth apply restricted driving license age 15. inconsistency defining ages transition child youth young adult. cultural contexts significant considering links education, employment health young new zealanders second half 20th century. ethnically, new zealand society became diverse period, two major changes migration pacific island peoples 1960s, new immigrants asian countries 1980s onwards. different levels educational attainment account disparities, diffidence difference also influence employment rates cultural minorities.sourceministry youth affairs, new zealand young new zealanders, wellington, statistics new zealand title: "Dear Dot" Children's Letters [Newspaper Column] - Children and Youth in History | New Zealand Childhoods (18th–20th c.)
content: teaching module new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.) jeanine graham, university waikato new zealand (retired) "dear dot" children's letters [newspaper column]annotationdesignated children's pages became quite common regional newspapers early 20th century, providing range stories, news items, illustrations, quizzes, poetry, competitions, occasional contributions children since 1886, however, pages written children headed "dot's little folk" appeared weekly otago witness, editorship william fenwick confused eminent older brother, george, controlled influential otago daily times). decade later, 1898, popular new zealand farmer magazine, edited gerald peacocke, developed similar children's feature section entitled "children's post office." fenwick peacocke took personal interest contributions youthful correspondents successful fostering sense club membership. "dot's little folk" ("dlf") received badges, chose pseudonyms supply full names addresses), frequently acknowledged community functions. "uncle ned" "children's post office" page farmer, "dot" would often print short response end correspondent's letter. commonly known time fenwick peacocke first "dot" "uncle ned" respectively. otago witness ceased publication 1932, otago daily times carried "dlf" tradition end decade. "dlf" contributors wrote country, though majority correspondents came farms small settlements lower south island. "children's post office" also colony-wide input. correspondents maori. costs paper postage, shyness, challenges writing second language may reasons cultural imbalance.sourcedot's little folk. otago witness, november 27, 1918, 57. annotated jeanine graham.primary source textdear dot,-my teacher took us glenorchy 11th get us used children examination. saw soldier boy, soldier boy's mate, mountain lily, forget-me-not. rough coming back launch, got soaking wet spray. examination put wearing glasses steamer ben lomond began whistle coming lake news peace came mum got cowbell got school bell, made great noise brother harry hen sitting 12 eggs. postman henry d.f.l. able come home won't dot? two nice little bay foals named peace victor. born day peace declared. peace white star forehead white nose. victor white star also, white hind foot. dear wee things. also two little puppies. names tot vaux. carry away anything get. paradise ducks wee ones lagoon present. hen one chicken 10 eggs. christmas far away close love mother's little helper, daddie's right hand, shepherd lad, mother's mate, beverly, mountain maid, soldier boy, forgetting dear self.-yours truly, mountain gentian (kinloch) dear dot,- hope l.f. free dreadful influenza. received badge sent hope won't lose like news armistice germany good, owing terrible epidemic, really cannot rejoice many people suffering distress. nice fine days lately, hope continue, think helps kill influenza. love l.f., also truly, tui (papatoetoe) dear dot,-i read d.l.f page every week long time, thought would like write 13 years old, left school. pony ride. season backward one, grass nice green ocean pearl, golden daffodil, tweedle-dee cousins. good many men gone war. glad peace declared, think everyone love d.l.f. yourself.-yours truly, lily oak (catlins) glad decided write page, lily oak, hope hear again.-dot.) cite source "new zealand childhoods (18th–20th c.)," children youth history, item #93, movement evolved international youth movement offered romantic program vigorous outdoor life boys adolescents cure physical decline social disruption caused industrialization urbanization. one scouting's main goals create social stability dealing complex problem adolescence. every generation fears generation comes respect rules, values, division property. uniformed disciplined youth organization, scout movement taught young males difficult years childhood adulthood respect older generations accept place society. 1920s nations world embraced movement way teach young people loyal state respect elders. governments worldwide utilized scouting reinforce political social authority, case colonial africa marginalized groups social outsiders used scouting challenge dominant institutions. scouting began 1907 robert baden-powell's creation youth organization aimed promoting physical, moral, imperial fitness among british youth capitalizing fascination "frontier woodcraft" "tribal" life. incorporated elements scouting order inspire young britons emulate interpreted praiseworthy aspects african life. diverse eclectic mix tribal peoples included amerindians, arab bedouins, new zealand maoris served inspirations movement, africans occupied central place baden powell's thinking. 20-year career fighting colonial wars made self-proclaimed expert "tribal" cultures, claimed incorporated scout movement. first, baden-powell specific ideology scouting. eventually several key themes emerged thinking became central core scout creed. concerned urban slums vice undermining british security, aimed prepare younger generations defend nation empire. life imperial frontier taught virility, resourcefulness, self-control, scouting "school woods" would instill ideals british youth. adopting values discipline "tribal" peoples, scouting would teach vital manly qualities consumerism materialism drained away "civilized" western society. similarly, baden powell also believed class tensions led national weakness. therefore envisioned scouting way teach working-class boys accept place society stressing obedience, discipline, simplicity. helps explain fourth scout law: scout brother every scout." baden powell never intended brotherhood lead social equality; rather sense fraternity scouting would defuse social tensions reducing friction rich poor boys. tense years world war one, movement's critics charged scouting secretly prepared young men military service. baden powell emphatically denied charge, war recast scouting international peace movement. significantly, also acknowledged non-europeans could also scouts gave blessing administrators educators introduced scouting throughout empire teach imperial loyalty, encourage african asian students accept place colonial society, reduce political social friction came foreign imperial rule. inter-war era, colonial administrators educators begun fear student unrest, urban migration, juvenile delinquency products growing social crisis local african communities. british administrators relied local allies chiefs govern african majority, worried younger generation's rejection elders' authority threatened widespread political social instability. boy scout movement promised correct imbalance teaching students city boys respect colonial authority throughout continent. french-speaking africa, baptist missionaries belgian congo tried substitute scouting secret male initiation ceremonies, considered immoral, catholic educators sought use movement train "christian knights" assist converting wider african population. similarly, french colonies authorities tried use scouting train small african elite would help control rest colonial society. eastern southern africa, british officials claimed authority african allies stemmed "tribal tradition." also introduced western schooling train young africans help run colonies demonstrate "civilizing" "primitive" subjects. scout movement never achieved mass african following, targeted students, juvenile delinquents, urban migrants greatest threat british rule. colonial educators administrators worried "detribalized" africans politically dangerous, particularly flaunted "tribal tradition" aspired live western lifestyle alongside european settlers. colonial authorities turned scouting "retribalize" african adolescents teaching remain countryside accept authority "native chiefs." ironically, looked scouting teach african boys "tribal." yet africans also used scouting claim rights full citizenship. invoked fourth scout law, declared scout brother every scout, challenge racial discrimination. rather making colonialism run smoothly, scouting offered african boys way resist discriminatory laws social barriers made second-class citizens. rejecting authority official colonial scout associations, formed unauthorized troops claim power legitimacy scout movement scouting thus instrument colonial authority subversive challenge legitimacy british empire. african scout experience thus demonstrated marginalized groups social outsiders could use movement challenge institutions. baden powell would dismayed independent troops twisted reinterpreted scout canon demand rights, respect, eventually independence. kenya, african troops ventured politics early 1950s supporting anti-colonial mau mau rebellion, essentially civil war landless kikuyu young men wealthy kikuyu chiefs landowners allied british colonial regime. african boys wanted join movement illegally acquired uniforms donned, others used scout clothing exploit colonial authorities' assumption trustworthy. dressed scouts, could travel freely colony often able collect money "scout" activities. scouting thus simultaneously bolstered colonial authority challenged legitimacy british empire. despite challenges posed 1950s, territorial scout associations africa grew prospered allying colonial authorities. european scout leaders demonized african nationalists caught surprise men came power independence early 1960s. seemed likely movement's close ties british imperialism would lead demise post-colonial africa, africans inherited control scout associations reinterpreted scout canon transfer loyalty new nationalist regimes. survival scouting nationalist era thus demonstrates movement's vulnerability re-interpretation outsiders also one great strengths. new lines political authority clear, scout associations made african nationalist regimes focus second law scout loyal"). even modern south african scouting, lost popular african support unwillingness challenge apartheid, successfully reinvented force economic social development new south africa. cite source "african scouting (20th c.)," children youth history, item #95,
