title: Legal Protection for Scout Uniform, 1935: Tanganyika Government Ordinance [Official Document] - Children and Youth in History | African Scouting (20th c.)
content:
teaching module african scouting (20th c.) tim parsons, washington university legal protection scout uniform, 1935: tanganyika government ordinance [official document]annotationmany african boys, teachers, community leaders genuinely inspired scouting founded unauthorized independent troops. cases, individuals dressed scouts claim benefits belonging movement. scout leaders government officials east africa paid little attention informal adaptations scouting, became alarmed dance societies mombasa began use scout uniforms early 1930s. decidedly adult celebrations often included drinking forms inappropriate revelry. scout leaders lobbied colonial authorities crack scout impersonators, time laws unauthorized use scout materials east african colonies. legislation tanganyika (modern tanzania) gave territorial scout association sole legal control scout uniform badges. 1935 africans impersonated scouts formed unauthorized troops faced stiff fine one month jail time hard labor.sourcetanzania national archives, dar es salaam, tanzania. acc 71. annotated tim parsons.primary source text

an ordinance protect activities interests boy scouts association tanganyika territory, 5th july 1935.

  1. 1. ordinance may cited boy scouts ordinance.
  2. 2. ordinance. "association" means boy scouts association incorporated royal charter granted fourth day january, 1912.
  3. 3. shall lawful person, rules association duly authorized entitled publicly wear, carry bear uniform, badge, token emblem rules specifically adopted use authority association could reasonably held imitation style manner convey impression person rules entitled wear, carry bear uniform, badge, token emblem.
  4. 4. person shall sell, offer sale, article bearing badge token emblem specifically adopted use authority association, could reasonably held imitation unless shall first obtained authority commissioner writing so.
  5. 5. shall lawful boy scout, otherwise thereunto lawfully entitled authorised, pretend pass arrogate authority, position powers claim act
    1. (a) member tanganyika territory police force, or
    2. (b) officer exercising police functions service native authority
    3. (c) agent officer government native authority tribunal.
  6. 6. shall lawful person form, organise, work connection concerned forming, organising, working connection corps body persons without due authority granted accordance rules association claim purport boy scouts otherwise connected association hold pass boy scouts otherwise connected association.
  7. 7. person contravening provisions ordinance guilty offence liable imprisonment one month fine two hundred shillings penalties.

title: "The Scouts' War Dance": Sir Robert Baden Powell's adaptation of a Zulu chant, c1910s [Chant] - Children and Youth in History | African Scouting (20th c.)
content: teaching module african scouting (20th c.) tim parsons, washington university scouts' war dance": sir robert baden powell's adaptation zulu chant, c1910s [chant]annotationlike much public turn-of-the-century britain, baden powell fascinated "primitive" cultures. although claimed expert knowledge africa service colonial wars, baden powell hardly authority zulu customs. matter, metropolitan britons almost entirely ignorant african institutions. nevertheless, fascinated romanticized depictions new colonial subjects popular press, juvenile literature, memoirs colonial war heroes. confident cultural superiority, british came believe african peoples like zulu preserved simpler, savage, nobler qualities seemed disappearing modern industrial society. baden powell built popular support scout movement tapping sentiments. claimed based scout ranks zulu age grades used ndebele "war horn" call scouts order. "scout war dance" combined professed zulu military chant "een-gonyama song") made dancing "be-prepared chorus." odd ritual sort thing edwardian schoolboys loved allowed play africans thoroughly modern context.sourcebaden powell, robert. scouting boys: handbook instruction good citizenship. 9th ed. london: c. arthur pearson, 1920. annotated tim parsons.primary source textscouts form one line leader front, holding staff right hand, left next man's shoulder. leader sings een-gonyama song. scouts sing chorus, advance front steps time, stamping unison long notes. centre scout] steps forward carries war dance, representing tracked fought one enemies. goes whole fight dumb show, finally kills foe; scouts meantime still singing een-gonyama chorus dancing ground. soon finishes fight, leader starts prepared' chorus. commence een-gonyama chorus, another scout steps ring, describes dumb show stalked killed wild buffalo. creeping stalking animal, scouts crouch sing chorus softly, gets fight beast, simultaneously spring dance shout chorus loudly. een-gonyama song sung spirited way, droned dismally like dirge. cite source "african scouting (20th c.)," children youth history, item #95,
